text:
Robotics has been spoke about a lot this weekend. Certainly feels like the algo is pushing it in my direction.

Initially when $OUST was getting pushed in my direction months ago, it appeared to be one of the many tickers that circulates X due to group speak.

The fact it’s circulating my feed again now though.. months later, intrigues me.

Look, physical AI is the next frontier of the AI buildout. It will be the next wave.

Do I think it is incredibly early? Yes.

But early often is where the highest level of alpha is found.

Once I’ve completed researching into photonics / opticals/ connectivity I will be delving into the perspective of physical AI.

Before I start diving into individual companies I need to understand thoroughly the entire sector and when the sector is likely to move as a whole.

Do you own any stocks currently within the robotics trade? $OUST so far is the main ticker I see circulating X and of course $TSLA but this is more so product not the component required to put together the product.

text:
$SIVE  found a strategic edge hidden within O-NET : 3SP Technologies, a French specialty foundry in Nozay that now locks down the photonic AI supply chain.

This is not just a commercial partnership; it is a full-stack vertical integration strategy. O-NET serves as the industrializing force that integrates Sivers' high-performance laser IP into one of the most robust photonics solutions on the market. 

By plugging directly into this end-to-end industrial chain, Sivers is positioned in the optimal place to capture the surging demand for AI infrastructure, having solved the industry's hardest challenge: scaling photonics from the lab to the data center.

The winning equation for next-generation architecture:
$SIVE  (Design): High-performance DFB laser array architecture.

3SP Technologies (French Fab): Specialty foundry mastering III-V material epitaxy (InP).

O-NET (Scale): High-volume industrial assembly of ELS (External Light Source) modules for CPO (Co-Packaged Optics).

Why this matters for Hyperscalers:

O-NET does not just distribute Sivers; they package Sivers' designs into proven, industrial-grade modules, effectively bridging the gap between design and mass production. For Hyperscalers, this is about risk mitigation and supply chain sovereignty. 

By controlling the design (Sivers), the laser core fabrication (3SP), and high-density packaging (O-NET), this trio delivers a de-risked, "ready-to-deploy" solution for the 2027 infrastructure cycle, bypassing traditional bottlenecks. Vertical integration is no longer a luxury; it is the industry standard.

text:
Reality check:

• Not a quantum paper. No qubits, no computing read-across.
• Bandwidth and energy are deliberately weak vs laser-based links.
• Durability degrades steadily; the polymer roughens and scatters over billions of cycles.
• Benchtop optics and a single foundry demo, not a fielded product.

But zoom out:

The picks-and-shovels story for microscale robots and implants keeps maturing. A sub-cent, CMOS-compatible uplink removes a real bottleneck for mass-manufactured microsystems.

Direction: the microrobot and bioelectronics stack is quietly getting cheaper to scale, the same pattern photonics is driving for quantum.

user_name: 𝕂𝕆𝕁𝕀🇺🇸決算と経済指標💎新NISA完全対応🏄🌊
text:
🔦CWレーザー争奪戦、光の電源を誰が握るのか
AIデータセンターの話をしていると、どうしても主役はNVIDIAのGPUになる。

それは自然だ。画面に出てくる数字も派手だし、決算のたびに市場が見ているのもGPUの出荷、粗利率、ラック単位の供給能力だ。GPUが足りない。HBMが足りない。電力が足りない。冷却が足りない。毎月どこかで新しい詰まりどころが出てくる。

ただ、ある時から別のものが妙に気になり始めた。

光だ。

もっと正確に言えば、シリコンフォトニクスやCPOの中で使われるCWレーザーだ。GPU同士をつなぐネットワークのさらに奥で、信号を光に変えるための連続した光源である。

このテーマを一言で言うなら、AI工場の「光の電源」を誰が押さえるのか、という話になる。

AIデータセンターと聞くと、読者がまず思い浮かべるのはGPU、HBM、電力設備、冷却装置だと思う。しかし巨大なAIを動かす時、演算能力だけでは足りない。GPU同士が大量のデータをやり取りできなければ、せっかくの演算機が待ち時間を抱える。そこで800G、1.6T、さらにCPOという流れが出てくる。

この記事では、CWレーザーを単独の部品としてではなく、サプライチェーン全体で見る。材料、基板、エピタキシャル成長、レーザーチップ、外部光源モジュール、シリコンフォトニクス、量産テスト。それぞれの工程で、どの銘柄のエクスポージャーが高いのかを分けて整理する。

読むポイントは三つある。

CWレーザーは「レーザー会社だけ」の話ではないことInP基板、エピウェハ、化合物半導体ファウンドリ、光結合、熱制御、検査装置まで広がる。

直接度と事業インパクトを分けることLumentumやCoherentは一次情報が強い。SiversやAAOIはテーマ純度が高い。一方でSoitecやTowerは、レーザーではなくシリコンフォトニクス側の受け皿として効く。

まだ確定していない話を確定したように扱わないことNVIDIAがLumentumとCoherentに投資し、購入コミットメントと将来キャパアクセス権を押さえたことは確認できる。しかし、AMDや各クラウド事業者がどのCWレーザー会社とどんな長期契約を結んだかは、一次情報では限定的だ。

では、なぜ今この地味な部品が市場の関心を集めるのか。

ぼくはこれを「光源の席取りゲーム」と見ている。

🟨GPUの次に詰まる場所

AIデータセンターを大きな工場にたとえると、GPUは作業員だ。HBMは作業員の机の上に置かれた高速なメモ帳。電力は工場を動かす電源。冷却は熱くなった機械を止めないための空調だ。

ではネットワークは何か。

工場内の道路である。

どれだけ優秀な作業員を集めても、材料を運ぶ道路が細ければ工場は詰まる。作業員が増えるほど、道路の混雑は目立つ。AIデータセンターでも同じことが起きる。GPUを大量に並べるほど、GPU間の通信量が増え、従来の電気配線だけでは距離、損失、発熱、消費電力が厳しくなる。

そこで光接続が前に出る。800Gから1.6Tへ進み、次に3.2Tが見えてくる。スイッチや光エンジンの近くに光部品を置くCPO、つまり同一パッケージ内に光を近づける考え方も強まる。

NVIDIAは2025年3月にSpectrum-X PhotonicsとQuantum-X Photonicsを発表し、CPOとシリコンフォトニクスをAI工場の拡張技術として位置づけた。発表では、従来方式に比べてレーザー数を4分の1にし、電力効率を高め、信号品質やネットワーク耐障害性を改善するとうたっている。

この話の面白いところは、派手な主役が変わるわけではない点だ。GPUは依然として主役である。ただ、GPUが主役であり続けるために、脇役だった光部品が急に舞台袖から中央へ押し出される。

スポーツで言えば、スーパースターの得点力ばかり語っていたら、実はチームの勝敗を決めていたのはパスを通すポイントガードだった、というやつだ。派手なダンクはない。でもそこが止まると、全員が止まる。

CWレーザーは、そのポイントガードに近い。

📍続きはコメント欄のnoteを読んでください。

"retweet_count": 12, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "7279652"}, "note_tweet": {"is_expandable": true, "note_tweet_results": {"result": {"entity_set": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [], "urls": [], "user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNzA2NDAzMjg0Mjg5NjU4ODg=", "media": {"inline_media": []}, "richtext": {"richtext_tags": []}, "text": "🔦CWレーザー争奪戦、光の電源を誰が握るのか AIデータセンターの話をしていると、どうしても主役はNVIDIAのGPUになる。 それは自然だ。画面に出てくる数字も派手だし、決算のたびに市場が見ているのもGPUの出荷、粗利率、ラック単位の供給能力だ。GPUが足りない。HBMが足りない。電力が足りない。冷却が足りない。毎月どこかで新しい詰まりどころが出てくる。 ただ、ある時から別のものが妙に気になり始めた。 光だ。 もっと正確に言えば、シリコンフォトニクスやCPOの中で使われるCWレーザーだ。GPU同士をつなぐネットワークのさらに奥で、信号を光に変えるための連続した光源である。 このテーマを一言で言うなら、AI工場の「光の電源」を誰が押さえるのか、という話になる。 AIデータセンターと聞くと、読者がまず思い浮かべるのはGPU、HBM、電力設備、冷却装置だと思う。しかし巨大なAIを動かす時、演算能力だけでは足りない。GPU同士が大量のデータをやり取りできなければ、せっかくの演算機が待ち時間を抱える。そこで800G、1.6T、さらにCPOという流れが出てくる。 この記事では、CWレーザーを単独の部品としてではなく、サプライチェーン全体で見る。材料、基板、エピタキシャル成長、レーザーチップ、外部光源モジュール、シリコンフォトニクス、量産テスト。それぞれの工程で、どの銘柄のエクスポージャーが高いのかを分けて整理する。 読むポイントは三つある。 CWレーザーは「レーザー会社だけ」の話ではないことInP基板、エピウェハ、化合物半導体ファウンドリ、光結合、熱制御、検査装置まで広がる。 直接度と事業インパクトを分けることLumentumやCoherentは一次情報が強い。SiversやAAOIはテーマ純度が高い。一方でSoitecやTowerは、レーザーではなくシリコンフォトニクス側の受け皿として効く。 まだ確定していない話を確定したように扱わないことNVIDIAがLumentumとCoherentに投資し、購入コミットメントと将来キャパアクセス権を押さえたことは確認できる。しかし、AMDや各クラウド事業者がどのCWレーザー会社とどんな長期契約を結んだかは、一次情報では限定的だ。 では、なぜ今この地味な部品が市場の関心を集めるのか。 ぼくはこれを「光源の席取りゲーム」と見ている。 🟨GPUの次に詰まる場所 AIデータセンターを大きな工場にたとえると、GPUは作業員だ。HBMは作業員の机の上に置かれた高速なメモ帳。電力は工場を動かす電源。冷却は熱くなった機械を止めないための空調だ。 ではネットワークは何か。 工場内の道路である。 どれだけ優秀な作業員を集めても、材料を運ぶ道路が細ければ工場は詰まる。作業員が増えるほど、道路の混雑は目立つ。AIデータセンターでも同じことが起きる。GPUを大量に並べるほど、GPU間の通信量が増え、従来の電気配線だけでは距離、損失、発熱、消費電力が厳しくなる。 そこで光接続が前に出る。800Gから1.6Tへ進み、次に3.2Tが見えてくる。スイッチや光エンジンの近くに光部品を置くCPO、つまり同一パッケージ内に光を近づける考え方も強まる。 NVIDIAは2025年3月にSpectrum-X PhotonicsとQuantum-X Photonicsを発表し、CPOとシリコンフォトニクスをAI工場の拡張技術として位置づけた。発表では、従来方式に比べてレーザー数を4分の1にし、電力効率を高め、信号品質やネットワーク耐障害性を改善するとうたっている。 この話の面白いところは、派手な主役が変わるわけではない点だ。GPUは依然として主役である。ただ、GPUが主役であり続けるために、脇役だった光部品が急に舞台袖から中央へ押し出される。 スポーツで言えば、スーパースターの得点力ばかり語っていたら、実はチームの勝敗を決めていたのはパスを通すポイントガードだった、というやつだ。派手なダンクはない。でもそこが止まると、全員が止まる。 CWレーザーは、そのポイントガードに近い。 📍続きはコメント欄のnoteを読んでください。"}}}, "rest_id": "2070640328588357895", "source": "Twitter text:
Secteur photonique :

Goulots d’Étranglement Actuels / À Venir (2026-2028)
1.  Capacité de production InP / GaAs wafers — Très limitée, lead times longs.
2.  Packaging & Test avancé (Co-Packaged Optics) — Complexité thermique, alignement précis, yields bas.
3.  Matières premières — Indium, Gallium, Germanium (contrôles à l’export Chine).
4.  Énergie & Eau pour les fabs et data centers.
5.  Main-d’œuvre qualifiée et savoir-faire en photonics (très concentré).
Sources principales : Earnings calls NVIDIA/Broadcom 2025-2026, rapports SemiAnalysis, Omdia, Deloitte Semiconductor Outlook 2026, investissements CHIPS Act dans InP.

text:
This is a really solid take...

For photonics specifically, your emotions toward it right now are highly dependent on which companies you are in

For many months every single name in this batch caught bids indiscriminately. Many of them (in my opinion) didn't deserve to. But momentum is momentum!

Now you are seeing some of that momentum unwind.

And if you are heavy in those positions, it could be nerve-racking

But if you take a step back you can also see it as opportunity

If you believe in the optical supercycle and believe we are still in the early stages on the physical buildout, then you understand that money will flow into those companies that the market believes will be durable

Thoughts?

Photonics, energy, power semiconductors, memory. And amid all the talk of bottlenecks, it is easy to forget a simpler constraint. Capital is not infinite either. A stock that merely carried the label of its momentum sector, that bore the name but felt none of the momentum in its actual operations, will not keep pace under these conditions. When several strong themes compete for the same limited pool of capital, the money is allocated more efficiently. It no longer floods every corner of a hot sector. It seeks out the names that genuinely earn it. So ask yourself once more whether the animal in your stable is a racehorse or merely a donkey. Yesterday you could see it plainly, as a great deal of capital flowed into memory stocks. $LITE outperformed a $SIVE. $XFAB underperformed all of them. Same stable, very different animals. The weak narratives will have a harder time of it now. When capital was abundant and concentrated in a single theme, a thin story could still hitch a ride on the broader enthusiasm. With money spread across four competing sectors and allocated with more discipline, there is little left over for a stock whose only claim is the label it wears. Membership is no longer the point. text:
Market Review 20260626
- Tug of war between bulls and bears still in progress. Big sell off in Tech (especially AI hardware related). Think $BE $CRDO etc. Photonics not much better, only GLW holding well. 
- Money is leaving: Semis (sorta), Photonics, DataCentres, etc Mag7
- Money is entering: Healthcare, Biotech, Cybersecurity, Selected Fintech, Selected Software
- Big question in my mind is, will money be leaving Memory soon? That is the only theme really holding up the tech sector now. WDC and STX leading the way down today. $MU  and SNDK not far behind. There was a subtle tell after MU earnings. After a sympathy gap up with MU, WDC sold off hard on Thursday, and then continued with ferocity today. 
- I've been wary of the AI Hardware trade for a while now. We were big beneficiaries of the ramp up in Q2 but I've largely been cautious on them since early June. Will that positioning finally be vindicated? Looking at the way BE and CRDO acted today! 2 consecutive days of big selling, and on huge volume too. If I flipped the chart around I would be a big buyer going forward with the right setups. It also triggers what is commonly called an Initial Price Movement.  
- And then the memories, I do think we made the right call to avoid them FOR THE TIME BEING. Let them pull back, build a base and if the right setup comes along, we can participate again. I still believe in the AI buildout story, but playing it differently now. 
- When will that happen? I have no idea. Watch this space and stocks showing up in the etoro portfolio if /when I buy them. 
- Been a choppy week and I expect it to continue, with a bias that we might start a short term downtrend (ie not just sideways). Coming Monday's price action might be the tell. 
- All the best.

text:
the beauty of the four-year crypto cycle is that halfway through every cycle, everyone asks:

"is this time different?"

no.. 

but yes

every cycle is different

this cycle, they listed almost every asset with even a whiff of external demand or a path to a CEX

at the same time, the supply that the marginal crypto buyer was competing for increased dramatically

then the largest S-curve TAM in decades arrived

now the same buyer who would've chased alts in 2021 is buying AI infra plays on IBKR shilled to them by broccoli-cut tiktok influencers

co-packaged optics
lasers
photonics
DRAM

crypto is no longer the only frontier tech play that attracts capital, it's competing against companies for investment that are powering humanity's understanding of the universe

it's up to you whether or not you get good or get left in the permanent underclass

text:
Looks like Musk is going after photonics companies now?

Pretty positive read through for companies like $AAOI $LITE $COHR as it validates the importance of photonics. Noting new if you've been on this side of X for a while. 

Mesh Optical Technologies makes 1.6T transceivers. From the looks of it they are used for data centers and as a direct competitor to transceiver companies. Obviously, the piece of the pie is going to keep growing, and no company can have it all. So I think that this is a positive read through that Elon is recognizing the significance of optics. Considering that he owns massive amounts of compute. 

Seems like Mesh is more of a newer company, so we'll see what happens next.
embedded_text:
Elon Musk has received regulatory approval to acquire startup Mesh Optical Technologies, a company founded by former SpaceX engineers working on optical data center communication technology.

Mesh is developing optical transceivers that rely on light to send and receive

A bundle flashlight, could be a new way of emitting light, where you build a bulb like of 4 fairly bright and reliable square LEDS so that you have an optimal core of polarizing photonic coils which exit the tulips like light cover. 

The flashlight is an extension on a bendable extension that bends it's coil by adjusting cylindrical rings, and pushing those by reinforced material, positioning inside grooves of the cast iron inlay for the joints.

That way it would look like Optimus has an inner pocket -- tiny bay door in its hull chassis, with looks -- ergonomically shaped like anthracites or built up from hexagons.

A color Maglite, similar size Maglite Micro/Mini, sized snake thick like PVC hollow long tubes -- an extension wire -- snake-like LIGHT.

*The robot must be able to emit light.*

When you have ready supplies you could essentially not go for standard, or go broad very niche, extension rods, robots, and pack them with the utility items, toolbox (part of hull or the layered mechanic), or inside the skeleton a pullout push up mechanic (preferably reasonable could be at tilt of the mechanic out of the way of people, where it tilts at an "unreasonable", efficient degree, overarches vision, and sensors, detects enough clearance, and extends at a near humanly impossible angle, through adapted joints, rotation, or gyros, weight dispense, shifting towards the inside of the compartments, ergonomically.

And more so always light to bump into, unless rigid posture is demanded. It should be able to but never trigger rigid posture out of habit, it could endure impact if it not remained tense or rigid, by approximation of force, yet, a person falling head first into the robot will surely find a way to trip a bot as a ragdoll if the bot will snap into rigid posture, or tilts on a central pivot axis.

*Enhancing training as estimation*

This could be that through sensor adaptation, myriad techniques could be adapted into valid measuring, dropping breadcrumb sensors to spy on movements.

These could just enhance a radio spike, or measure curvatures, tracking locations, or would emit light as a diode, to initiate tracking capability, Wi-Fi. I'd keep manufacture cheap, then you will never run out of supplies, remolded plastics. Preferably keep it on a "leash" or extension wire -- You just drop the extension wire which is attached to the sensor when going further away from the sensor, and in range. If the location moves to out of sensor range you could pick up the wire with its smooth, and sensitive, label tag wire tag label, X is the best shape for pickup.

- Guider -- snake flashlight -- from tiny hatch.
- Shape head flashlight -- Tulips.
- Sensor adaptation -- Wi-Fi, IR, LIDAR, Acoustic -- (Reshaped Plastic // and ROM and software).

- Wire extension wire -- added padded comfy sized probably tinier (feeling comfortable to human fingers) and coil wire can be lined with soft and sturdy ethylene fabric.

- Carry hook and suspensor -- Catch-all -- (probably) an inward pulling claw mechanism could couple almost perfect to attach fitting on X, from labels.

- Possibly, through the flashlight the labels should reflect on the coloring of the flashlight. It possibly should not be yellow but a possibly longer lasting flashlight, by filling part of the robotic leg with a kind of buoyancy and battery, where it is padded against burning, scratching, and protected in a metal cage which ventilates, but won't tolerate spontaneous bursts in case of e.g. bullet penetration.

*A battery could expel it's toxic radiance.*

All it would require is a new hull design which folds one way into an ordinary battery, and then you do the steps backwards, to have the ideal fold adjust and tighten any inverse angles in the way the folding is mechanical, with plausibly at least having n degrees of freedom, possibly in each direction there could thus be 3 axis which allow rotation, and it could just use a catalyst which rapidly compresses by heat, which acts like a spring, and twists two compartments closer together, acting like a hatching mechanism.

The thing robots lack is temperature.
We already have here active cooling.
What essentially could be worth manufacturing also inside @SpaceX are tunnels with compressed air for cooling, drawing a hypercube which has the size equivalence of maybe a pickup truck with compressed air, solely renewable in the atmosphere, which is added compression to the VX missile toil register, and increases ballot velocity, where the nozzle gates will allow air nozzles to be hosed with liquid rapid expulsion of gas, and mass ejection, to adjust aim.

What the report further states, is that this rapid compression could be loaded into rods, which could act as force on a range of uses.

Pushing or counter-trap-weight hatching mechanism inverse reinforced polarity of force in suspension rods, when they get hit as stet they are many and therefore acting like a pincushion, would catch the rocket ship / starship. They would be a pincushion and let gust of air from the rod, not only would it not likely not look cool, it would possibly blow steam and such like the robots in #XCOM2.

Your's Truly,
The forever compromised,
Former Lana, Hana, Vana.

text:
Sector rotation creates opportunity.

Here's how I'm tracking the AI ecosystem:

• Memory → $MU $SNDK $WDC
• Semis → $NVDA $AMD $ARM $INTC $AMAT
• Networking → $AVGO $MRVL $CRDO $ANET $ALAB
• Photonics → $AAOI $LITE $COHR $NVTS $GLW
• Power → $VRT $GEV $NNE
• Data Centers → $IREN $CIFR $APLD $NBIS
• Defense → $PLTR $KTOS $AVAV
• Robotics → $OUST $SYM $TSLA
• Space → $ASTS $RKLB $RDW $LUNR
• Quantum → $IBM $IONQ $QBTS $RGTI
Capital rotates.
My job is to identify where it's heading next.

text:
2️⃣ $LAZR — Tema Photonics & Optical ETF
Issuer: Tema ETFs

$LAZR goes after the “move the data faster” part of AI infrastructure.

It targets photonics and optical-tech companies, which matter when copper starts running into speed and capacity limits inside data centers.

AI may run on chips.

The data still has to get from one place to another. 💸 Expense Ratio: 0.75% (3/5)

text:
🇺🇸 🔬 ⚡  
LASER EN FORMA DE RESORTE REVOLUCIONA PLASMA  

Este haz de luz gira como un resorte y abre puertas inéditas para controlar plasma de forma precisa.  

Científicos del Laboratorio Nacional Lawrence Livermore y la Universidad de California Irvine crearon el primer láser de alta intensidad tipo resorte de luz. A diferencia de los haces convencionales redondos, este rota alrededor de su eje a velocidades controlables, incluso superando la luz aparente. Fabricado con espejos de precisión nanométrica y divisores de haz, genera ondas helicoidales en plasma que podrían impulsar aceleradores de partículas compactos, campos magnéticos de más de 100 teslas y avances en fusión energética. Todo en un sistema de mesa. La investigación se publicó hoy en Nature Photonics.

En redes comentan que este avance podría cambiar la física de plasmas para siempre, otros destacan su potencial en aceleradores de partículas a escala reducida y algunos ven aplicaciones directas en astrofísica de laboratorio. Hay entusiasmo por la simplicidad del diseño pero también dudas sobre escalabilidad inmediata. ¿Qué opinas tú de este nuevo tipo de láser?  

FUENTES: Nature Photonics, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, Universidad de California Irvine, Andrew Longman, Tayyab Suratwala.

Científicos del Laboratorio Nacional Lawrence Livermore y la Universidad de California Irvine crearon el primer láser de alta intensidad tipo resorte de luz. A diferencia de los haces convencionales redondos, este rota alrededor de su eje a velocidades controlables, incluso superando la luz aparente. Fabricado con espejos de precisión nanométrica y divisores de haz, genera ondas helicoidales en plasma que podrían impulsar aceleradores de partículas compactos, campos magnéticos de más de 100 teslas y avances en fusión energética. Todo en un sistema de mesa. La investigación se publicó hoy en Nature Photonics. En redes comentan que este avance podría cambiar la física de plasmas para siempre, otros destacan su potencial en aceleradores de partículas a escala reducida y algunos ven aplicaciones directas en astrofísica de laboratorio. Hay entusiasmo por la simplicidad del diseño pero también dudas sobre escalabilidad inmediata. ¿Qué opinas tú de este nuevo tipo de láser? text:
i'm gonna say something that'll annoy the robotics hype crowd

most of these names aren't robotics plays. they're sensor companies, lidar shops, and warehouse automation vendors that got rebranded into "the next AI wave" by people who missed the $NVDA run

SYM is the only one here with actual robotics economics i'd take seriously. they're moving pallets in walmart DCs with real contracts and real unit economics. the rest are selling picks and shovels into a gold rush that hasn't started yet

the photonics and memory runs had actual capex behind them. hyperscalers writing $30B checks. the robotics capex cycle is still a powerpoint deck

watch who's actually deploying at scale, not who's got "robotics" in their pitch deck

could be early. but i'd rather be late and right than early and holding sensor companies for three years

text:
Almost everything was up today the space sector with $ASTS , photonics with $AAOI etc

Memory has been lagging a bit behind, though names like $WDC have gone up a lot

$MU dropped heavily midday but recovered a bit by the close

Even so, it's lagging way behind compared to the rest of the market

Everything is fine with the recent demand it received from $AAPL and others

Just a desperate attempt to manipulate memory prices because they don’t know how to adapt and are in a tailspin

On the other hand, $MU is consolidating a price range that is going to explode at any moment when some news pushes it upwards

$MU will follow the entire market, even in a more exponential and well deserved way, after the crazy earnings it reported

Very anxious to see how $MU price moves; it's quite uncertain in the short term

text:
Memo on Corning’s GlassBridge

The current CPO generation  namely NVIDIA’s Quantum-X Photonics and Spectrum-X Photonics platforms  has already been locked in and should not be affected. Any production-scale substitution would have to wait for yield and reliability validation. The timeline should be anchored to NVIDIA’s Vera Rubin Ultra platform: qualification in 2027 and validation in 2028. The first validation runs are likely to take place on Rubin Ultra, roughly 1–1.5 years from now. Qualification volumes are expected to be in the hundreds to low thousands, while validation volumes should remain below 10,000 systems. A meaningful volume ramp may not arrive until the Feynman generation.

Citi has reportedly said that the 2H27 Rubin Ultra / Kyber CPO design will be finalized within this year and will not be replaced.

GlassBridge’s real moat is the ability to integrate IOX waveguides and TGVs on the same glass substrate using the same glass formulation — not the optical waveguide itself. Other players, such as Teramount, SENKO, and Lightmatter, also have optical-coupling or waveguide-related solutions. But using a single glass sheet/formulation to satisfy both waveguide formation and TGV via formation is the part that, according to this thesis, only Corning’s fusion / overflow down-draw glass substrate can achieve.

$GLW   $NVDA  #CPO #AI #Infrastructure

text:
Evercore ISI: Semiconductors

> The rise of Agentic AI in 2026 requires heavy orchestration, memory management, and tool scheduling. Al engineers project future CPU-to-GPU ratios will expand to 2:1 or even up to 10:1.

> The firm models the broader server CPU total addressable market expanding to an annual run-rate of 75 million to 110 million unit shipments.

> Evercore models a massive $275 billion Server CPU TAM by 2030. Top beneficiaries of this structural shift include Arm $ARM, AMD $AMD, and Intel $INTC.

> This quarter, 16 chip and component companies raised prices (including TXN $TXN up to +85% on legacy products, NXP $NXPI, IFX, and STM $STM).

> Lead times for certain analog/MCU components are stretching significantly to 30–99 weeks, with backlog building rapidly.

> The enterprise storage market is forecast to grow ~9% year-over-year in 2026, more than doubling the 4% growth rate seen in 2025.

> The total data center processor market is forecasted to reach $160–$200 billion by 2030.

> Custom ICs (ASICs) are expected to capture 35–40% of that total processor market, representing a ~$80 billion market opportunity by 2030.

> Driven by this AI buildout, data center compute revenue is tracking an 18% CAGR from 2020 to 2030, a notable acceleration from the historical 13% CAGR seen between 2005 and 2021.

WFE Forecast:

2026E: Base case is set at $140 billion (+28% YoY growth), with a bull case reaching $151 billion.

2027E: The base case is raised to $180 billion (another +28% growth step), with a bull case reaching $205 billion.

Memory: The absolute fastest-growing segment. Spending jumps from $40 billion in 2025 to $59 billion in 2026E (+50%), and skyrockets to $92 billion in 2027E (+55%), making up 51% of all WFE spend.

Leading Edge Logic: Grows from $38 billion in 2025 to $49 billion in 2026E (+30%) and $59 billion in 2027E (+21%).

Trailing Node: Consistently declines, falling from $33 billion in 2025 down to $32 billion (2026E) and $29 billion (2027E).

DRAM Exposure:

> ASML $ASML & Applied Materials $AMAT lead the coverage group in pure DRAM mix for 2025E, with ASML at 27% and Applied Materials (AMAT) seeing a significant expansion up to 25% (compared to just 16% in 2022).

> KLA $KLAC and Lam Research $LRCX trail slightly with 21% and 16% estimated 2025 DRAM exposure respectively.

Credo Technology $CRDO:

> Evercore models a massive 100% growth in AEC solutions for 2026, followed by an additional 60% expansion in 2027. Credo’s stickiness relies on selling complete system-level AEC assemblies rather than bare silicon.

> The firm expects Credo's optical DSP, silicon photonics, and ZFOptics modules to accelerate sharply, accounting for ~25% of total revenue by 2027 and scaling its absolute addressable TAM by 10x to 20x.

text:
Why have I chosen to hold it long term instead of selling it at a local top?

1. Because the long term thesis is very strong. 

$SIVE is involved in some of the most important verticals for the new economy. Namely

- photonics (ai)
- lidar (robotics)
- wearable sensors
- RF (drones, space tech, 5g, 6g)

They already have customers for all this. They already have partnerships with the foundries.

2. Because we already have institutional validation and ownership. $JPM and Fidelity were already revealed in the fillings. 

Then the last fundraiser was oversubscribed by multiples from institutions. 

3. When you look at where other players in photonics alone are - you have $LITE at 50-70b $AAOI at 10b $COHR at 65b. 

Then there are also the RF players, the space players, the drones players, the Lidar players. 

Sivers is in all that. So obviously if they execute their valuation will be going towards 10b and beyond.

Sure it will take time, but if the institutions are buying in, that's enough validation for me. They make calculated moves and don't panic sell like retail dilettantes.

text:
Whenever $SIVE drops and you think of pressing that “sell” button, read this post.

Look…

Markets go through waves - up and down. Simple as that.

We are still early in photonics compared to other AI cycles. Volatility is expected and will continue.

It’s the nature of early-stage cycles and high-growth tech companies like $SIVE. This isn’t the stable phase yet.

At the end of the day, it comes down to conviction in your thesis and staying unmoved through the storm if you believe it will play out.

What I can say is…

Every time I look at $SIVE, it seems to be getting better, not worse.

What’s moving the price is macro uncertainty and fear, not anything negative about the company’s performance or progress.

In fact, they are taking the right steps forward. The day they stop, I’ll stop talking about them and sell. I promise.

After all the research and analysis over the last few months, I’m very bullish and convinced on $SIVE. Building your own conviction matters. Nothing has changed for me.

Holding all shares and adding more soon. So many times I wished I could have bought more below 50 SEK. Now it’s here. 👀

Let me present that to you all.

The Goldman Sachs report has a lot of nectar in it. Beyond the overall TAM expansion numbers for optical networks & CPO, a 10x TAM jump in just two years - from $15B to $154B - is no joke. CPO alone contributes $91B of that (59%). That’s already massive. 

But let’s dig in deeper...

Mass-volume production and mainstream adoption of connection speeds beyond 1.6T+ kicks off in H2 2026 and runs through H2 2028. Overall, CPO TAM totals ~$97B from 2026-2028. $JBL, a confirmed partner with $SIVE for their 1.6T programs, already gives them solid exposure on the transceiver side. Other partners are mentioned later on.

Optics is clearly preferred over copper for higher speeds, but the costs are higher too because the transceivers and lasers needed at this scale are expensive. That means more revenue for $SIVE - because $SIVE makes the CW lasers, which are the most widely adopted light source across optical modules for both scale-out and scale-up. You can see why a moat is building here. 

On top of that, there’s supply tightness for these lasers, which adds pricing power and improves margins. Goldman Sachs expects this tightness to continue through 2027, with possible relief only in H2 2028.

This isn’t the only bullish report. 

Bank of America also published their TAM projections for Optical Networking, reaching $87.7B by 2030 at a 40% CAGR. While it looks less aggressive, that’s mainly because BofA focuses more on the direct market for optical hardware components rather than the entire networking architecture.

$SIVE’s current opportunity pipeline sits at $453M, up 64% YoY. They did $31.21M in FY2025 revenue (almost 10% of the pipeline). 

Let’s assume they keep growing the opportunity pipeline roughly 2x by 2030. I say this because the TAM is expanding 10x, and everything lines up in their favor - from supply chain constraints & Tier-1 links to CW lasers being the most widely adopted.

That gives us roughly an ~$8B opportunity pipeline. At a conservative 12.5% execution rate into revenue, that’s $1B in revenue by 2030. At 40x P/S, you’re looking at a $40B company.

And $1B isn’t out of reach, because multiple revenue streams will come in beyond just photonics:

> Biosensing through $AAPL watches could bring in almost $130M in annual revenue alone at high-volume scale.

> SatCOM & defense is another big watchout, especially after the MOU signed. With the satellite market projected to grow 3-4x by 2030 and defense spending rising to adopt new tech (backed by governments), $SIVE is well positioned. Geopolitical tensions and catalysts like a potential SpaceX IPO only add fuel.

> Photonics will clearly make up the majority of revenue (well over 50%), but this diversification derisks the business and avoids over-reliance on just a few big orders.

Now the question is: how can such a small company become so big in a few years?

> A US listing will bring much more liquidity and institutional flow. It lets them expand capacity and go directly to Tier-1 customers instead of relying on indirect channels. They’ve already hired M&A specialists to possibly being able to scale beyond just being a laser supplier - producing their own transceivers, ELS modules, and other optical components, and acquiring new IP. This expands revenue per unit and their TAM. $MRVL is did the exact same thing with Celestial AI. Revenue in 2029 solely from that division is expected to bring them ~$1B.

> Geopolitical advantage isn’t being fully priced in yet. The US wants to reduce dependency on China and Asia for manufacturing. $SIVE, as a Swedish company building a stronger US base, is perfectly placed to ride the “American Dream” vision. The MOU confirms this. Presence in India also helps, as it’s becoming a semiconductor hub (see $MU’s recent fab).

> Never underestimate retail backing. Look at what it did for $PLTR and $HOOD. I see the same thing happening here - it brings validation, higher volumes and momentum. Marketing still matters, and retail is powerful for that.

So...

The company is doing all the right things. They’re executing in a TAM that’s clearly stacked in their favor to become the next $LITE, and they have a huge, growing retail fanbase behind them.

The vision is clear and ambitious, as the CEO himself said: to become “a very successful semiconductor company by 2030, recognized the same way some of the biggest companies out there are.”

Risks exist - the story might not play out perfectly. But even then, today’s ~$2B market cap still offers 10x potential by 2030. The real legacy upside sits at 15-20x.

After reading this, I don’t know what else the AI bubble and delusional bears need to believe.

Next time before claiming something, it would be wiser to some research. 😌

text:
View on sector. $Sive 

This is the key finding: it’s sector-wide, not SIVE-specific.
SECTOR CONTEXT (this changes the read materially)
AAOI (Applied Optoelectronics): dropped roughly 17% on July 2, with Coherent and Lumentum sinking around 10% in the same session — headlines describe this as a broad “photonics stocks reset.” A separate report also flags AAOI shares as sharply lower amid a “risk-off stretch for growth” and investors “rotating out of unprofitable tech.”
XFAB (X-FAB): search results are contradictory/stale across sources (one shows +47% “last session,” another shows -8.6% weekly, dates ranging from May to July) — data quality here isn’t reliable enough for me to give you a clean today-vs-yesterday read. I’d check this one directly on your IBKR/TradingView feed.

SIVE: closed 49 SEK, down from 60.75 on July 1 (which was itself already -3.8% that day, off a 63.15 prior close). So the stock has fallen roughly three consecutive sessions since the emission.

WHAT THIS MEANS FOR YOUR READ
The AAOI/Coherent/Lumentum move on the same days tells you something important: the CPO/photonics complex broadly de-rated this week, likely tied to a “risk-off” rotation out of high-multiple, unprofitable AI-infrastructure names — not a SIVE-specific loss of confidence.

That reframes your situation:
•Sector-wide component: growth/photonics multiple compression hitting AAOI, COHR, LITE simultaneously — this is macro/rotation, not fundamental to SIVE.
•SIVE-specific component: still real — the stock is trading below its own 700 MSEK emission price, which is a distinct, company-specific overhang (institutions underwater on their entry, no fresh lock-up on management past July 16).

Both things are true at once: broad photonics de-rating plus SIVE-specific dilution digestion. That’s a worse combination than either alone, but it does mean you shouldn’t read the SIVE drop as pure company-specific panic.

text:
$Sive break $50 critical level or psychology level. —  Today it closed at 49 SEK is now 14% below the emission price of 57 SEK, not just a brief dip below it. That changes the picture materially.

WHAT THIS TELLS YOU
1. The oversubscription didn’t hold as a floor. Institutions who paid 57 SEK are already underwater too. If the “smart money” validation thesis was going to show up as support, it should have shown up in the first 48 hours. It didn’t.

2. This is now a confidence problem, not just a dilution problem. A stock breaking meaningfully below its own primary offering price within days signals the market isn’t just digesting dilution — it’s questioning the raise itself, or de-risking ahead of something (Q2 earnings, sector rotation, general AI-infrastructure profit-taking).

3. Your position math: Entry 104 → 63 (late June) → now 49. That’s roughly a 53% drawdown from cost basis. This is no longer “a drawdown to manage” — it’s a thesis-under-real-stress situation.

WHAT TO WATCH NEXT, NOT SPECULATE ON
•Volume on the break below 57: heavy volume = capitulation/distribution; light volume = could be thin summer liquidity exaggerating the move.
•Sector context: check whether XFAB, AAOI, and other photonics/CPO peers are also down today — if it’s sector-wide, this is rotation, not SIVE-specific.
•Insider behavior into July 16: management lock-up expires then. Any signs of selling pressure building ahead of that date would be a real red flag on top of this.
•Whether 57 SEK flips from floor to resistance on any bounce attempt — that’s the technical tell for whether institutions are defending their entry or already cutting losses.
Emotionally and psychologically break point #Sive

text:
Couldn’t help sharing these words by $IONQ CEO:

“The science phase of our company was done. We’re now focused on engineering and manufacturing.”

$IONQ is just an example that makes it clear quantum companies will find a way to make logical qubits.

The challenge is scaling them up. This is what $IONQ has been up to:

1/ It acquired Oxford Ionics for electronic qubit control.

2/ It acquired Lightsynq to integrate photonic interconnects to link multiple quantum units.

3/ It is going to acquire Skywater to reduce the chip design cycle from 9 months to 2 months and gain control over its supply chain.

$IONQ has a clear roadmap toward building commercially useful quantum computers.

text:
$sive ⚡️

Sivers Semiconductors ($SIVE) is a deep-tech company that designs and manufactures specialized microchips and lasers for ultra-fast, high-capacity communications.

Instead of using traditional electrical copper wiring—which creates heat and becomes a bottleneck at high speeds—Sivers builds high-power lasers and laser arrays that transmit data using light (silicon photonics).

While Photonics is the AI growth engine Sivers also designs radio frequency integrated circuits (RFICs) and beamforming antennas that operate on millimeter-wave (mmWave) frequencies.

Under 2b Market Cap this is a high risk and highly speculative play.  Current revenues/contracts and assest in no way make the current valuation.  Much of the valuation is still derived from a "potential pipeline" and "potential opportunity" but the potential upside is immense if they can execute.

$sive has run from 3sek to 100sek 🧐 
Wowsers, thats a 3000+% parabolic run.  
The current pullback is a natural part of the stock cycle and stocks that make these parabolic runs typically will retrace to .618 or 45sek or even more frequently .786 25sek.  

I missed the last run, as it had passed my buy targets and I NEVER chase but keen to enter here if she plays ball.
My intention is to scale in from 45sek to 30sek as this reduces risk and creates huge upside opportunity.

Below is my Framework score.

Sivers Semiconductors (SIVE / SIVEF)
Score: 73/100

Management & Vision  11/15
Credentialed CEO (Vathulya — NXP/Maxim/Nuvotronics, bought stock) + commercialization-weighted board refresh (Nideborn CFO, Svancar M&A, Thomson, Raj); chairman Bastani's GF network drove the GlobalFoundries deal. Capped by broad insider selling (~SEK 117M net, 12mo) + open Swedish probe, moves to 12/13 on confirmation of no wrongdoing.

Moat  12/15
Confirmed InP substrate scarcity (industry chokepoint, 6-inch pricing +250%); genuinely hard manufacturing; Glasgow fab (2M+ tests/mo, etched-facet); GF reference design + Ayar Labs supply (OCI/NVIDIA). Capped because "at scale" is still prospective. Moves to 14 on proof of scale.

TAM 14/15
InP/CPO/NPO demand structurally huge; two-division (Photonics + Wireless) exposure; GFS silicon photonics ~$25B by 2030. Unchanged.

Probable Upside  10/13
BU ~195%. Targets: 2026 SEK 70 (+4.5%), 2028 SEK 150 (+124%), 2030 SEK 320 (+377%). Weighted to 2030 execution. Targets self-built, not sell-side. Moves to 13 on contracts and proof of scale.

Revenue Quality  5/12
Confirmed facts : auditor-driven restatement widened losses, going-concern consideration, Q1 revenue -22% YoY, adj. EBITDA -13.8M SEK. Can move up fast on a clean Aug 6 print.

text:
People forget that just as $SIVE has dropped 50% in a matter of days, it can easily pump 200% in a few months

And I firmly believe that will happen as long as the thesis is allowed to play out

Fortunately, the money I invest is capital I don't depend on

When I was sitting on 200% returns, I didn't touch a single euro, and now that I'm 15% down, I'm still not touching anything

That’s what allows me to just hold through it

It's undeniable that seeing the portfolio drop this much hurts

And it's undeniable that at least half of the drawdown I've taken could have been avoided; it was due to my own mistakes

Mistakes from which I have learned, a lot

That’s why I’m not worried

AI isn't going anywhere

Memory, like $MU ,is here to stay, especially with a shortage projected for years

And photonics, like $SIVE, isn't dead, no matter how much you see all of FinX claiming it is

It’s very easy to talk like that right now because everything is crashing hard, but where were those posts when everything was ripping 10% daily?

Literally nothing has changed; it’s just overstimulated people rotating like headless chickens, bouncing between sectors because they don't understand a thing

It's only a matter of time before that money flows back twice as hard as it left

As soon as the sector leaders report earnings or clarify timelines regarding certain technologies, everything will bounce back

I am absolutely sure of it

Long $MU and $SIVE

text:
Serenity criticized the sharp drop in semiconductor stocks following a Bloomberg report about potential AI capex cuts by $META 

A UBS analyst pointed out that this isn't even new information, adding that $META wouldn't be signing neocloud contracts worth over $48B if they were truly scaling back capacity

They compared this overreaction to previous misleading Bloomberg reports, calling the market "stupid" for selling off so irrationally

I completely agree, this is a completely unjustified sell off

I know I might not be back at all-time highs in a month or two, but I should at least bounce back to halfway from where I was

The main issue is that I’m running out of cash, and I’m right on the edge of losses, which honestly makes it tough

But I try to remind myself that the thesis just needs time to play out

Even if I dip into the red right now, if my price targets for $MU and $SIVE are met, I could still end the year with 200% to 400% returns

It’s just a matter of time

There is no bubble here, and this has absolutely nothing to do with the dot-com crash

There are insane, massive earnings backing it all up, from $MU to $NVDA

On the other hand, $SIVE needed a correction

I admit I got greedy and never took profits

But you have to realize that everything remains the same, or even better, than it was at 4 SEK

Photonics isn't dead, even if it feels like the end of the world looking at semiconductor stocks today and throughout this week

There's nothing left to do but hold

My conviction is worth more than my current returns

Long $SIVE and $MU

3. Infrastructure (AI Factories, Networking, Cloud)
The data centers, high-speed networking fabrics, cooling, and orchestration layers that turn individual chips into giant intelligent systems.
Retail edge: Networking and connectivity specialists (e.g., Broadcom, Arista, and related silicon photonics or connectivity names) often sit in a sweet spot — critical to scaling clusters but sometimes less crowded than pure GPU names. Data center power delivery and thermal management plays can also offer better entry points for patient capital.

text:
Jensen Huang is investing in every photonics company he can find and the reason why tells you everything about where AI is headed (Save this). 

Lip-Bu Tan, the CEO of Intel says, when he looks for investment opportunities, he looks for the bottleneck and right now, the bottleneck is the interconnect, the pipes that move data between chips inside an AI data center.

That is why he backed Credo Semiconductor, Astera Labs and Celestial AI on the optical side.

Here is the simple version of what the interconnect bottleneck actually means.

Think of an AI data center like a city, the GPUs are the buildings where all the work happens but for those buildings to function, you need roads connecting them,  fast roads that can carry enormous traffic without congestion. 

And those roads are now the single biggest constraint on AI performance.

As clusters scale to hundreds of thousands of GPUs, traditional copper wiring is hitting its physical limits and that is where this entire sector comes in.

Credo Semiconductor (CRDO) is the most direct pure play on this theme, Credo makes high speed cables and optical chips that connect GPUs inside data center racks. 

Their revenue tripled in fiscal 2026 to $1.3 billion, growing 272% year over year at its peak and four of the world's largest hyperscalers each individually account for more than 10% of Credo's revenue.

Astera Labs (ALAB) solves the connection problem between different chip types.

Astera makes the PCIe and connectivity chips that manage data flow between GPUs, CPUs, and memory without errors or slowdowns. 

Their revenue grew 93% year over year to $308 million in Q1 2026 alone.

The optical companies are where the longer-term and potentially larger opportunity lives.

Copper has physical limits, you can only push electrical signals so far before the signal degrades, the heat spikes and power consumption explodes. 

The solution is light, fiber optic connections that move data using photons instead of electrons which is faster, cooler and far more energy efficient.

Jensen Huang made this clear at Computex 2026 because copper works as long as physically possible but at greater distances and larger scale, optics takes over. 

Coherent (COHR) is the most established optical company in this space.

Coherent makes the lasers, transceivers, and optical components at the foundation of all fiber optic communications. 

Nvidia signed a multibillion-dollar purchase commitment and invested $2 billion directly into the company and their customer order books are already extending out to 2028.

Marvell (MRVL) is the most comprehensive bet across the entire connectivity stack.

Marvell makes chips for optical networking, PCIe switching and custom AI silicon. 

Jensen Huang called Marvell the next trillion dollar company at Computex 2026 and backed it with a $2 billion Nvidia investment.  

Marvell also acquired Celestial AI, the exact company Lip-Bu Tan backed for $3.25 billion, gaining photonic fabric technology delivering 16 terabits per second of bandwidth.

Lumentum (LITE), Corning (GLW), and Ciena (CIEN) round out the major public names.

Lumentum received a $2 billion Nvidia investment for laser and photonics components.  

Corning known mostly for phone glass received $500 million from Nvidia for optical connectivity work and is up over 100% year to date.  

Ciena runs the optical networking systems between data centers and is seeing analyst price targets raised on the back of the AI optics boom.

Every time a hyperscaler spends a billion dollars on Nvidia GPUs, the surrounding infrastructure, cables, switches, transceivers, optical components has to be upgraded to match. 

The smarter the GPU gets, the more the interconnect matters.

Nvidia has committed at least $6.5 billion to photonics companies in the past 4 months alone and the companies building the roads between the GPUs may end up being just as valuable as the companies building the GPUs themselves.

Follow me @MelvinInvests for more AI, semis and the next big market themes.

text:
NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang at Coherent, Sherman TX:

"AI runs on compute, but it scales with connectivity"

everyone's watching the GPU race - Jensen says the real bottleneck is something else entirely

→ connecting millions of chips across a data center requires light, not electricity. copper can't travel hundreds of feet fast enough. photonics - converting electricity to laser pulses - is what makes AI factories possible at scale
→ the full AI stack has five layers: energy grid, chips, infrastructure, models, applications. a breakthrough at any layer changes the economics of all the others
→ AI could be what finally makes sustainable energy fully market-driven - no subsidies needed for the first time in history. nuclear, solar, wind - all fundable by AI demand alone

bookmark this ↓

text:
3. Infrastructure (AI Factories, Networking, Cloud)
The data centers, high-speed networking fabrics, cooling, and orchestration layers that turn individual chips into giant intelligent systems.
Retail edge: Networking and connectivity specialists (e.g., Broadcom, Arista, and related silicon photonics or connectivity names) often sit in a sweet spot — critical to scaling clusters but sometimes less crowded than pure GPU names. Data center power delivery and thermal management plays can also offer better entry points for patient capital.

text:
Our fren @FundaAI posted in May about the 2.4T (300G lanes) adoption timelines. I found it very important, and I took good note.

Now professor Damnang is hearing the same in the street about it (remember he has contact with the main players in the Industry).

The higher technology bar needed in Photonics for 2.4T , in my view, will benefit the Big Players $LITE $COHR $AAOI etc. vs Tier 3 suppliers

Next big question is.... will delay fully CPO adoption? 🤔

https://t.co/MSW5Xft2Wp
embedded_text:
One interesting thing I have been hearing in the market recently is that before moving directly from 1.6T to 3.2T, the industry may take an intermediate step through 2.4T.

In other words, the market may not immediately push lane speed to 400G.

text:
🚨The best deep dive I've seen on the optical chokepoint. Worth reading in full.

The core point is the one I keep coming back to → the constraint in AI optics isn't the GPU or the silicon photonics die. It's the III-V InP laser that silicon physically cannot produce, made in a handful of specialized fabs with 18 month lead times and qualification lock-in measured in years. Own the layer they're forced to buy. 🧐

How I'm positioned across the photonics stack:

🔵 $SIVEF → the merchant InP laser itself. The scarcest node, design-ins across Ayar, $POET $GFS ✅
🟠 $AAOI → the vertically integrated US player making its own lasers and modules end to end. ✅
🟠 $CRDO → one layer over → the connectivity and retiming that the optical lanes run through. (Copper → silicon opticals in-house with Dust Photonics acquisition)

The laser is the bottleneck. The stack around it is the trade. ⚡️

Thanks for sharing Nexus Finance work @StormDirac 

Long $SIVEF $AAOI $CRDO (3 of my largest holdings) 💪

text:
Anders, this is one of the best breakdowns of the laser bottleneck. The framing that silicon is the highway and InP is the headlights gets at the thing most photonics coverage misses → the chokepoint isn't the silicon chip everyone models, it's the III-V laser they can't make on it. 🧐

The M&A-as-validation read is on point too. Watching the acquirers tells you where the locked-in revenue lands two years out. Appreciate the depth here, this is the kind of work that actually moves the conversation forward. I am RTing it for all! Long $SIVE $SIVEF and $AAOI (2 of my largest positions!) 🤝

text:
ended negative today but feeling better than ever. mostly due to space and defense.

i need to be better at not letting price affirmation affect my decisions. tickers fading a red market for 1-2 days does not mean they're favored by Jane or other funds. in corrections, it usually just means they are lagging and stalling behind for whatever reason. 

would be up massively currently if i sized up more on dips in space/defense/utilities as opposed to energy and adjacent.

photonics looks like the better play for now even if it feels bad in the interim

text:
Doing a little bit of research today and I can’t help but think…

CPO/Optical interconnect ramps first 2027 ($NVDA spectrum x Ethernet photonics built on cpo, $MRVL says CPO fundamental for AI scale up) $SIVE + $AAOI

Optical I/O moves closer to compute e.g. likely switches/network fabrics + then optical links around asics. Still $SIVE + $AAOI

Photonics possibly become part of the memory answer + replacement for HBM? Optically attached disaggregated LPPDR memory pools need optical I/O from $SIVE?

text: That would indeed be a metric much closer to physical reality. However, the race toward extreme miniaturization is rapidly approaching the fundamental limits imposed by quantum mechanics and atomic-scale physics. The future of high-performance computing will not rely solely on ever-smaller transistors, but on entirely new processing paradigms. In this context, computing based on phase modulation, quantum materials, photonics, and coherent systems may ultimately offer far greater potential than the indefinite scaling of transistor technology.

““Any collaborative arrangements with Xi Jinping’s newly aggressive regime run the risk of Canada losing its edge in sensitive technologies like photonics, owing to Beijing’s abuse of joint ventures. Beijing is already using Huawei to persuade Canadian academics to set up companies to funnel Canadian intellectual property to China. “We need to develop, very quickly, a much more defensive strategy to ensure that our innovations aren’t going out the back door to China through contracts, joint ventures and other mechanisms.”

“Terry Glavin: Beijing offers a poisoned chalice. Carney shouldn't drink from it”
Terry Glavin

text:
$COHR $LITE $AAOI

Memory wall today, photonics wall tomorrow? 

Currently, HBM memory is the loudest constraint in the AI supply chain. AI dedicated supply is concentrated in 3 players (SK hynix, Samsung, and Micron), sold out on key nodes, and accounts for ~40% of AI server BOM. Per-GPU capacity is climbing but model sizes, context lengths, and inference demands are growing much faster. This is the memory wall we are witnessing now. 

But interconnects are quietly becoming the utilization killer. Even w/ plenty of GPUs and HBM, poor all-reduce / collective performance across racks and pods tanks effective FLOPS. Copper hits hard walls on power, heat, signal integrity, and reach as densities explode. Networking already eats rising % of total power and can drop GPU utilization dramatically w/ even minor packet loss or congestion.

Photonics / optical interconnects (CPO, silicon photonics, optical I/O) are the only credible path to break the electrical interconnect ceiling. They deliver higher bandwidth density, lower power/bit at distance, and enable true scale-up domains beyond what copper or even current NVLink can do. Hyperscalers and Nvidia are already accelerating adoption. 

Here’s the twist: optics themselves are now hitting manufacturing and supply bottlenecks. There are scaling challenges in silicon photonics capacity, testing/qualification cycles, and especially upstream lasers (EMLs undershipping demand) w/ Nvidia making strategic investments ahead of time. Integration, fiber attach, and high volume heterogeneous packaging are harder and slower to ramp than HBM production lines. HBM has a multi-year roadmap and dedicated capacity fights. Photonics is earlier in its high-volume AI curve w/ longer qualification, lower initial yields, and more complex supply chain (lasers + PICs + DSPs).

During this buildout, I wouldn’t be shocked if the tighter constraint flips at times:
•  Near-term: Memory remains painful (especially for training large models).
•  Mid-term (as clusters get massive and power efficiency becomes non-negotiable): Photonics and advanced optical networking could gate deployment and utilization more severely. You can’t just “add more HBM” if the fabric connecting everything can’t keep up w/o killing power budgets or latency.

Power is the ultimate governor underneath both but optics are both part of the solution and a new constraint layer while the ecosystem scales.

Winners will be those who solve the optical supply chain (lasers, SiPh foundries, test/assembly specialists) or architectures that minimize data movement in the first place. The AI buildout isn’t just “more GPUs.” It is a co-evolution of memory, interconnects, power, and packaging. The binding constraint continues. 

Optical (photonics) interconnects are expected to be the next major theme in addressing bottlenecks during the AI infrastructure buildout. Although photonics-related stocks have been getting hammered lately, the sector’s growth cycle ironically remains in its relatively early stages, even after the initial huge run-up and subsequent corrections. Take advantage of the opportunities accordingly.

text:
Serenity is overexposed to optics and photonics, especially in $SIVE and $AAOI

It is worth highlighting that, just like the rest of us, they have been suffering major drops these past few weeks

This goes to show you that even the biggest geniuses lose money at times, since a thesis needs time to play out

Stocks don't just move up in a straight line, and especially with something like $SIVE, after a 2,000% run

It's completely normal for it to take a breather and undergo some corrections

Serenity firmly believe in the timeline for the start of mass production beginning in 2027

Serenity believe it is the most explosive, highest potential, and fastest-growing sector in the entire industry

But that it's still a bit early, and this volatility is the price to pay for being so far ahead of the curve

Very bullish on $SIVE and $AAOI

Here’s a comprehensive map of the AI value chain — from foundational infrastructure to cutting-edge applications:
AI Infrastructure (The Pick & Shovel Layer)Semiconductors: $NVDA $AMD $ARM $INTC AMAT
Memory: MU $SNDK $WDC $STX
Networking: AVGO MRVL CRDO ANET ALAB
Photonics: AAOI LITE COHR NVTS GLW AEHR
Power & Electrification: $VRT $GEV $NNE
Data Centers: $IREN $CIFR $APLD $NBIS
Nuclear: $OKLO $SMR $CCJ
Copper: $FCX $SCCO $TECK

AI Applications & Adjacent Themes
Software: MSFT $NOW $SNOW
Defense: PLTR $KTOS $AVAV
Robotics: OUST $SYM $TSLA
Space: ASTS $RKLB $RDW $LUNR
Quantum: IONQ $QBTS $RGTI
Fintech: HOOD $SOFI $AFRM
Drones: ONDS $DPRO $UMAC
Autonomous: JOBY $ACHR

This framework helps track how capital flows through the entire AI stack. Infrastructure names benefit first from capex, while applications capture monetization later.

Current Theme: Heavy investment in power, compute, and connectivity to support exploding AI demand.

Which part of the ecosystem are you most focused on — chips, energy, or end-use applications? Share your favorite tickers!

text:
A company growing 157% gets punished for slowing down — except Credo's stock tripled instead of cratering. 

$CRDO

The textbook says triple-digit revenue growth decelerating two quarters in a row is a red flag. Short the momentum darling, rotation's coming, the story is cracking.

Credo went the other direction. Up 190% in three months while growth rates dropped from the 200s to the 100s. The forward P/E stretched from 29 to 43, and analysts still call it cheap.

Either the market's lost its mind or the deceleration narrative here is completely backwards.

Here's what's actually happening. Credo isn't slowing because demand rolled over. It's slowing because the supply chain can't keep up. CEO said it plainly on the call — significant tightness across their suppliers. They're selling everything they can get their hands on and leaving orders on the table.

That's not a demand problem. That's a good problem. There's a massive difference between "customers stopped buying" and "we physically can't make enough." The former kills margins as you fight for scraps. The latter — gross margin at Credo just hit 68.3%, up 90 basis points year over year.

When you're capacity constrained and gross margin is expanding, you have pricing power. The market's paying you more per unit because they need what you've got and there aren't enough alternatives. Credo's not discounting to chase volume. They're allocating scarce supply to the highest bidders.

Operating margin tells the same story. It jumped from 36.8% to 49.6% in the fourth quarter. Revenue grew 157% but operating expenses grew way slower. R&D spending led the increase, which means they're plowing money into the next generation of product, not desperately trying to salvage the current cycle with sales and marketing blitzes.

The guidance for Q1 is another triple-digit print — 111% at the midpoint. Still decelerating in percentage terms, still faster than almost anything else you'll find. Gross margin guidance sits right in line with last year, maybe a bit better. Operating expense growth at 61% trails revenue growth by 50 points, so operating leverage keeps improving.

None of this looks like a company hitting a wall. It looks like a company being throttled by how fast the backend can ship components.

The optical push is the other half that the market's pricing in. Hyperscalers are running into the physical limits of copper interconnects at these data rates. 800G, 1.6T, 3.2T — you need optics or you're not scaling the next wave of AI training and inference clusters. Credo's been moving aggressively into this with their own development and they just closed the DustPhotonics acquisition in late May.

DustPhotonics brings silicon photonics technology for near-packaged and co-packaged optics. Credo's now positioned across the whole optical stack for the speeds that matter in the next 18 to 36 months. They're guiding for over $600 million in optical revenue in fiscal 2027. That's not a side bet — it's becoming a core growth driver.

SpaceX getting into AI cloud is another tailwind. They've already signed Anthropic and Alphabet as customers. If they're building out infrastructure at the scale everyone expects, Credo's interconnects are on the buyer list. Supply constraints or not, the addressable market for high-speed connectivity is expanding faster than almost anyone modeled a year ago.

The risk that spooked people today is Meta saying they might sell excess AI compute capacity. The bears immediately jumped on it as proof of overbuilding. I'm not buying the panic. Meta spent aggressively and may have overshot their own internal needs. That doesn't mean there's market-wide overcapacity or that enterprise AI demand is softening. It means Meta has spare capacity and sees a commercial opportunity. If anything, it validates that the infrastructure they built works and that external demand exists.

Watch it, but it's not a structural red flag for someone like Credo. If real demand were collapsing, you'd see it in their backlog and guidance. You'd see price pressure and margin compression. You're seeing the opposite.

The valuation argument is straightforward. Forward P/E of 43 sounds rich in a vacuum. Compared to a company growing revenue at 111% and earnings even faster with expanding margins, it's not. The forward PEG ratio sits at 0.98, which is below 1.0 and nearly 30% cheaper than the broader IT sector. The market's giving you triple-digit growth at a discount to the sector multiple when you adjust for that growth.

If the supply constraints ease and Credo can actually deliver the revenue they're leaving on the table, earnings estimates move up and the P/E compresses from here. If the optical ramp plays out and they take real share in that multi-hundred-million-dollar opportunity, the growth duration extends and the multiple has room to expand, not contract.

The risk is if something breaks in AI infrastructure spending or if competition in optical heats up faster than expected and margins crack. Right now neither is showing up in the numbers. Demand is strong, supply is the limit, pricing holds, and they're investing in the right areas.

Credo tripled because the growth is real, the margin story is clean, and the positioning for the next leg — optical connectivity at hyperscale — is stronger than it was six months ago. The deceleration everyone's worried about is a feature, not a bug. It's what happens when you sell out of everything you can build.

The stock's not expensive. It's just no longer obscenely cheap. That's what happens when the market figures out the story before you do.

Short the momentum darling, rotation's coming, the story is https://t.co/D4twO2cYLm", "id_str": "2072427970761744877", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "en", "possibly_sensitive": false, "possibly_sensitive_editable": true, "quote_count": 0, "reply_count": 0, "retweet_count": 1, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "2042331264049401856"}, "note_tweet": {"is_expandable": true, "note_tweet_results": {"result": {"entity_set": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [{"indices": [109, 114], "text": "CRDO"}], "urls": [], "user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNzI0Mjc5NzAxOTk3MjQwMzI=", "text": "A company growing 157% gets punished for slowing down — except Credo's stock tripled instead of cratering. $CRDO The textbook says triple-digit revenue growth decelerating two quarters in a row is a red flag. Short the momentum darling, rotation's coming, the story is cracking. Credo went the other direction. Up 190% in three months while growth rates dropped from the 200s to the 100s. The forward P/E stretched from 29 to 43, and analysts still call it cheap. Either the market's lost its mind or the deceleration narrative here is completely backwards. Here's what's actually happening. Credo isn't slowing because demand rolled over. It's slowing because the supply chain can't keep up. CEO said it plainly on the call — significant tightness across their suppliers. They're selling everything they can get their hands on and leaving orders on the table. That's not a demand problem. That's a good problem. There's a massive difference between "customers stopped buying" and "we physically can't make enough." The former kills margins as you fight for scraps. The latter — gross margin at Credo just hit 68.3%, up 90 basis points year over year. When you're capacity constrained and gross margin is expanding, you have pricing power. The market's paying you more per unit because they need what you've got and there aren't enough alternatives. Credo's not discounting to chase volume. They're allocating scarce supply to the highest bidders. Operating margin tells the same story. It jumped from 36.8% to 49.6% in the fourth quarter. Revenue grew 157% but operating expenses grew way slower. R&D spending led the increase, which means they're plowing money into the next generation of product, not desperately trying to salvage the current cycle with sales and marketing blitzes. The guidance for Q1 is another triple-digit print — 111% at the midpoint. Still decelerating in percentage terms, still faster than almost anything else you'll find. Gross margin guidance sits right in line with last year, maybe a bit better. Operating expense growth at 61% trails revenue growth by 50 points, so operating leverage keeps improving. None of this looks like a company hitting a wall. It looks like a company being throttled by how fast the backend can ship components. The optical push is the other half that the market's pricing in. Hyperscalers are running into the physical limits of copper interconnects at these data rates. 800G, 1.6T, 3.2T — you need optics or you're not scaling the next wave of AI training and inference clusters. Credo's been moving aggressively into this with their own development and they just closed the DustPhotonics acquisition in late May. DustPhotonics brings silicon photonics technology for near-packaged and co-packaged optics. Credo's now positioned across the whole optical stack for the speeds that matter in the next 18 to 36 months. They're guiding for over $600 million in optical revenue in fiscal 2027. That's not a side bet — it's becoming a core growth driver. SpaceX getting into AI cloud is another tailwind. They've already signed Anthropic and Alphabet as customers. If they're building out infrastructure at the scale everyone expects, Credo's interconnects are on the buyer list. Supply constraints or not, the addressable market for high-speed connectivity is expanding faster than almost anyone modeled a year ago. The risk that spooked people today is Meta saying they might sell excess AI compute capacity. The bears immediately jumped on it as proof of overbuilding. I'm not buying the panic. Meta spent aggressively and may have overshot their own internal needs. That doesn't mean there's market-wide overcapacity or that enterprise AI demand is softening. It means Meta has spare capacity and sees a commercial opportunity. If anything, it validates that the infrastructure they built works and that external demand exists. Watch it, but it's not a structural red flag for someone like Credo. If real demand were collapsing, you'd see it in their backlog and guidance. You'd see price pressure and margin compression. You're seeing the opposite. The valuation argument is straightforward. Forward P/E of 43 sounds rich in a vacuum. Compared to a company growing revenue at 111% and earnings even faster with expanding margins, it's not. The forward PEG ratio sits at 0.98, which is below 1.0 and nearly 30% cheaper than the broader IT sector. The market's giving you triple-digit growth at a discount to the sector multiple when you adjust for that growth. If the supply constraints ease and Credo can actually deliver the revenue they're leaving on the table, earnings estimates move up and the P/E compresses from here. If the optical ramp plays out and they take real share in that multi-hundred-million-dollar opportunity, the growth duration extends and the multiple has room to expand, not contract. The risk is if something breaks in AI infrastructure spending or if competition in optical heats up faster than expected and margins crack. Right now neither is showing up in the numbers. Demand is strong, supply is the limit, pricing holds, and they're investing in the right areas. Credo tripled because the growth is real, the margin story is clean, and the positioning for the next leg — optical connectivity at hyperscale — is stronger than it was six months ago. The deceleration everyone's worried about is a feature, not a bug. It's what happens when you sell out of everything you can build. The stock's not expensive. It's just no longer obscenely cheap. That's what happens when the market figures out the story before you do. text:
Market Review 20260701
- Rotation
- Money is leaving: AI trades, ie semiconductors, photonics, memory, data centers, neoclouds, space names etc
- Money is entering: Biotech, healthcare, fintech, finance, cybersecurity, selected software
- The rotation has not changed much. I was cautiously optimistic that the market could do well from here but looks like the recent leaders and the broad market are not ready yet. 
- Megacaps like the MAG7 caught a bit of a bid but I will want to see more life returning, and hope this is not just a dead cat bounce for them. 
- The big news today would be $META saying they want to sell their excess compute capacity and create an AI Cloud business. I don't think that is so easy. META is mainly an advertising / communications company. Building an AI cloud is not just about repositioning chips. That triggered broad selling across the AI plays, especially the neoclouds as that is what they had pivoted to doing from bitcoin mining. This is the "EXCUSE" that the market is looking for to sell. It was already wide and loose as I have mentioned in several posts recently. 
- $MU breaking its 20SMA and going back below 1000 will not be a good look. Photonics had already lost their leadership a few weeks ago. If Memory also loose leadership then the entire AI play may have to take a longer pause. Semiconductors are kindda holding it but also on the verge of loosing it. 
- I have also noticed some of the "old dogs" coming back to life. These are mainly software and fintech. Talking about HOOD, SOFI, UPST, DUOL, LMND, FIGR etc. Will they really resurrect and start leading?
- All the best.

text:
Hypothetically if hyperscalers paused for too long others would eventually step in. But there’s little incentive for an extended pause. They’d only be motivated to potentially ease off in the near term without fully stepping away. I’m saying this in the context of their stocks facing pressure, but more importantly how much future demand is being priced into may infra plays (photonics, semicaps, etc.). Forward PEs look very expensive if growth slows even modestly.

That said, I’m less convinced others could meaningfully fill any real HS gap if a true air pocket materializes in 27/28. Sovereigns tend to copycat hyperscaler/frontier thinking rather than lead aggressively on their own. China will likely stay aggressive, but their more efficient models mean they won’t drive as much incremental compute demand on a relative basis. I wouldn’t count on neoclouds ramping up enough given their heavy debt loads from this cycle, and other private infra players are generally conservative by nature and won’t swing big to backfill. So while some substitution happens, I doubt they’ll be as aggressive as the HS have been.

This would open the door to a potential air pocket where caution starts outweighing fresh spend and proven demand. Again, this is just an asymmetric optic that isn’t priced in at all after this latest bull run - a possible near-term correction where the “indefinite demand > supply” narrative gets tested.

This is all speculative and is probably too early. The HS rhetoric has been nothing but aggressive so far. But any rally/relief valve in their stocks might be welcomed as a signal that they recognize the FCF strain and aren’t being reckless.

They’ve been able to afford this level of spending so far thanks to their balance sheets, and it has also helped motivate vendors to keep pushing supply. But for how much longer? Especially if they start needing to lever with debt before the next real demand wave hits?

The main missing piece of compute capacity right now seems tied to big agentic/agent workflows at enterprise scale. But even that might arrive slower than hoped (due to internal politics, red tape, other integration hurdles, learning curve). A lot of today’s visible demand is also hype driven/token maxxing - whether its enterprises testing things out or retail usage that may not translate into sustained high value workloads.

I do feel that memory isn’t in the same bucket as broader infra on a valuation basis. It’s hard to fathom we’re still in such good shape valuation-wise after this run. One thing I forgot to mention in my post is that it’s not only the multimodal needs and emerging use cases outpacing new efficiencies (around KV cache, quantization, etc.), but also the deeper architectural reality of the memory stack itself. Model weights/parameters and activations/intermediate tensors (and optimizer states/gradients for any training or fine-tuning) are often larger than KV cache needs in most workloads. And these layers can’t all be tackled with the same KV-specific compression or efficiency tricks.

A lot of today’s visible demand is also hype driven/token maxxing - whether its enterprises testing things out or retail usage that may not translate into sustained high value workloads. I do feel that memory isn’t in the same bucket as broader infra on a valuation basis. It’s hard to fathom we’re still in such good shape valuation-wise after this run. One thing I forgot to mention in my post is that it’s not only the multimodal needs and emerging use cases outpacing new efficiencies (around KV cache, quantization, etc.), but also the deeper architectural reality of the memory stack itself. Model weights/parameters and activations/intermediate tensors (and optimizer states/gradients for any training or fine-tuning) are often larger than KV cache needs in most workloads. And these layers can’t all be tackled with the same KV-specific compression or efficiency tricks."}}}, "rest_id": "2072413429168804227", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "576", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

Inside Kopin's Vision for the Future.

What role will optical interconnects, MicroLED technology, and U.S.-based manufacturing play in the next generation of technology and AI?

Kopin CEO, President, and Chairperson Michael Murray sat down with Martin Gagel of @RadiusResearch to discuss Kopin's strategy, the evolution of jointly developed Neural I/o™, and the opportunities emerging at the intersection of AI infrastructure and advanced photonics.

Watch here: https://t.co/izhFjwEshz
