text:
$AXTI $COHR Do you believe this is last InP prepayment deal that will be cut over the next 5 years? We merely getting started.
———————-
BOTTOM LINE: This is incrementally bullish for COHR fundamentals but more strategically important than financially material. It confirms that 6-inch InP substrate supply remains a gating item for COHR’s AI optical ramp, validates AXT/Tongmei as a meaningful 6-inch InP substrate supplier, and shows COHR is using customer-style capacity prepayments upstream to lock supply through the next 3 years. It should be more stock-moving for AXTI than COHR, but for COHR it is a positive de-risking datapoint after a weak tape day, not a thesis-changing event by itself.

The agreement is straightforward but important. AXT-Tongmei entered a 3-year Master Development and Supply Agreement with Coherent, effective June 25, 2026, covering “mass development and supply” of 6-inch indium phosphide wafer substrates. AXT will expand capacity at its Beijing facility from 2026 through 2028, Coherent will make a $22.2885M prepayment for committed capacity, wafer prices are pre-established, and the prepayment will be applied against future product purchases. Coherent also gets refund rights if unused amounts remain at expiry or termination, and can terminate and recover unused prepayment if AXT fails to meet the capacity commitment for more than 6 successive months. AXT gets protection if Coherent misses minimum order quantities, in which case unused prepayment becomes nonrefundable and AXT can terminate. AXT must also offer additional capacity beyond the committed quantity to Coherent on the same terms. The full agreement will not be available until AXT files its Q2 2026 10-Q.  

The key interpretation is that this is not about outsourcing COHR’s differentiated InP device manufacturing. AXT is supplying 6-inch InP wafer substrates, the upstream crystalline substrate input. Coherent still does the value-added photonic device work: EMLs, CW lasers, photodiodes, PICs and related integration. That distinction matters because COHR’s moat remains process/yield/device manufacturing and vertical integration; AXT is enabling more wafer starts, not replacing COHR’s 6-inch InP fab advantage.

For COHR, the positive read is supply-chain de-risking. Management has repeatedly framed InP capacity as the key constraint in the AI optical cycle, while also saying 6-inch InP is central to the revenue and gross margin ramp. In the May earnings call, COHR said its 6-inch platform was producing EMLs, CW lasers and photodiodes, with yields exceeding 3-inch lines, first 6-inch-derived transceiver shipments already contributing to revenue and margin, and internal InP capacity expected to double ahead of plan and more than double again by the end of calendar 2027.   This AXT agreement strengthens confidence that substrate availability will not become the hidden bottleneck that prevents those internal wafer starts from scaling.

This also lines up with the J.P. Morgan investor-meeting takeaway that COHR had substrate supply “locked in” across 5 suppliers spanning multiple geographies, several capable of producing 6-inch substrates. The AXT filing effectively names 1 of those upstream supply arrangements and shows there is real money, fixed pricing, and committed capacity behind the company’s prior substrate comments.  

The less bullish read is that the deal confirms the bottleneck is real and persistent. Coherent would not be making take-or-pay-like commitments and prepaying a supplier for 3-year capacity if 6-inch InP substrates were abundant. Reuters recently reported that China’s InP export controls have disrupted the AI photonics supply chain, that AXT and Sumitomo dominate global InP substrate production, that AXT is a major supplier to Coherent, and that 6-inch InP wafer prices have reportedly risen sharply since China tightened export licensing.   The agreement helps COHR, but it does not eliminate geopolitical risk because the capacity expansion is at AXT’s Beijing facility, precisely the jurisdiction where export licensing and materials controls have become a live issue.

The deal is financially immaterial to COHR in isolation. COHR’s market cap is roughly $65B and the $22.3M prepayment is de minimis against the equity value, capex program, and cash balance. It is best viewed as a capacity reservation / working-capital deposit, likely recorded as a prepaid asset and then consumed through substrate purchases, not as an immediate P&L event. COHR was trading near $333 after a roughly -9.6% move on July 2, so the stock reaction is more likely to be driven by AI hardware sentiment, valuation, and semiconductor tape than by a $22M supplier prepayment.

For AXTI, however, the signal is materially stronger. AXT reported Q1 2026 revenue of $26.9M, so a $22.3M prepayment is roughly 83% of a single quarter’s reported revenue, even though it is not immediately recognized as revenue and the total contract value could be meaningfully different from the prepayment. AXT also said in April that it had completed a $632.5M capital raise to support Tongmei’s InP capacity expansion and 6-inch InP R&D, so the COHR contract provides named strategic-customer validation for that capital deployment.   AXTI was down roughly -12.9% on July 2, and this after-close filing could support a rebound when regular trading resumes.

The most important subtlety is that the $22.3M should not be treated as the total revenue opportunity. The filing says the prepayment is applied against purchases and ties to committed capacity; it does not disclose wafer volumes, per-wafer prices, shipment cadence, gross margin, or whether additional capacity is expected to be purchased beyond the initial commitment. Using Reuters’ cited $5,000 average 6-inch InP wafer price as a purely illustrative benchmark, the prepayment would equate to roughly 4,500 wafers, but that calculation should not be used as a volume estimate because actual contract pricing is confidential and the prepayment may only represent a portion of the broader commitment.  

The fixed-price element is meaningful. If spot 6-inch InP pricing continues to rise, Coherent has partially hedged substrate cost and capacity access. That matters for the gross margin bridge because COHR’s >42% non-GAAP gross margin path relies on 6-inch cost/yield benefits, higher 1.6T mix, internal sourcing, and better absorption. J.P. Morgan’s May conference note highlighted 42%+ gross margin tailwinds from the shift from 800G to higher-margin 1.6T, the transition from 3-inch to 6-inch wafers, and movement from external to internal sourcing.   The AXT agreement supports that framework, but the benefit likely shows up gradually through wafer starts, device output, module shipments, and margin conversion rather than immediately in the next quarter.

This is also a useful read-through to AI optical demand. Coherent is not just receiving long-term customer commitments; it is passing those commitments upstream. That is how a real supply-constrained hardware cycle behaves. Customers commit to COHR, COHR commits to AXT, AXT expands Beijing 6-inch capacity, and the entire chain becomes increasingly governed by deposits, capacity reservations, fixed prices, minimum orders, and termination protections. This reinforces the view that the AI optical cycle is capacity constrained rather than purely speculative order inflation, though it does not disprove some double-ordering risk.

The agreement also has a China-risk wrinkle that investors should not ignore. Coherent has emphasized U.S. manufacturing scale, Sherman 6-inch InP leadership, CHIPS-related support, and the NVIDIA strategic partnership. This AXT agreement reveals that even a U.S.-anchored 6-inch InP device strategy still depends on upstream substrate inputs from China. That is not inherently negative, because AXT is a scaled and strategically important substrate supplier, but it means export permits, U.S.-China policy, force majeure language, and qualification of non-China supply remain critical diligence items.

Relative implications are clear. For COHR, this is a positive data point for execution confidence and supply visibility, but not enough on its own to revise estimates. For AXTI, it is a much bigger validation event because it confirms that a leading AI optical platform company is willing to prepay for Tongmei 6-inch substrate capacity. For LITE, the read-through is mixed: positive for the industry because InP scarcity is validated, but potentially negative at the margin if COHR is locking a larger share of scarce AXT 6-inch output. For the broader optical ecosystem, the deal supports the view that the scarcity value sits not only in finished transceivers but also deep upstream in compound semiconductor substrates, lasers, isolators, PM fiber, ELS modules, and other enabling components.

Trading implication: this should help stabilize the COHR narrative after the July 2 selloff, but it is not large enough to single-handedly re-rate the stock. The better near-term setup is probably AXTI up more sharply on customer validation and COHR modestly supported, with the caveat that U.S. markets are closed Friday, July 3 for Independence Day observed, so regular-session price discovery is delayed.   COHR remains a high-conviction structural AI optical beneficiary, but the stock still needs gross margin progression, 6-inch output conversion, and FCF inflection to justify the valuation. This announcement improves confidence in the supply-chain bridge to that outcome.

Key diligence questions for Monday/next week: whether this AXT capacity was already included in COHR’s “5 substrate suppliers” commentary or is incremental; whether export licenses are already secured; what share of FY2027 6-inch wafer starts this covers; whether the agreement includes first-priority capacity rights or merely committed capacity; whether the fixed wafer prices are below current spot; how quickly AXT can qualify volume 6-inch wafers to COHR specifications; and whether any of the prepayment is ultimately customer-funded through COHR’s own strategic LTAs.

text:
🔔 The Daily Semi-Cap — July 2, 2026

📊 SMH: $592.29 (-4.54%)

📈 Earnings:
• No new semiconductor earnings released today.

🔥 Major Developments:
• AXT-Tongmei signed a three-year 6-inch InP wafer substrate capacity agreement with Coherent, including a $22.3M prepayment tied to expanded Beijing capacity from 2026-2028. The optics supply chain is moving from spot-market scramble to locked capacity, which matters for Coherent, AXT, and the broader AI photonics bottleneck.
• Anthropic is exploring a custom AI chip with Samsung as a potential manufacturing partner, evaluating Samsung’s 2nm process and advanced packaging. If it moves forward, the read-through is bigger than one AI lab: frontier model companies are trying to control silicon roadmaps, and Samsung is still hunting for a marquee AI foundry win against TSMC.
• Nvidia is offering smaller GPU cloud providers financial backstops and revenue-share structures tied to unused GPU capacity. That supports near-term accelerator demand, but it also makes the AI infrastructure stack more circular: chip demand, cloud financing, and end-customer utilization are becoming increasingly intertwined.
• Meta said AI agent development over the last four months has not accelerated as expected, adding pressure to the capex ROI debate after reports that it may sell excess AI compute capacity. The takeaway is not that AI demand is dead; it is that hyperscaler capex now needs cleaner monetization proof, not just bigger buildout numbers.

text:
Hello darkness my old friend. 

Right when I think we’re done, you find a way to lure me back in!

I made peace with cutting $AXTI yesterday and it somehow finds a way to drag me back in. 

This deal is huge and the pre payment alone is nearly as much as their entire Q1 revenue. 

Not only that but it’s the catalyst it needs to shake off the recent fear around the AI infra trade and specifically photonics. 

This is my fifth time trying to trade AXTI in the past month. Is this time different?

text:
Fiber & Infrastructure Highlights – July 2, 2026

Conexon Connect Georgia completed its ninth FTTH project with Satilla REMC, surpassing 240,000 homes/businesses, 5,200 fiber miles, and delivering multi-gig to ~60,000 locations across 9 counties. Vocus announced ~A$500M ducted Sydney-Melbourne long-haul (6,912 cores, ready 2029, AI-driven). Cloud Capital/Realty Income formed a $6B data center JV (Virginia). South Korea outlined $919B mega projects with 18.4GW data centers by 2035. Nixxy/Tachyon announced a 1GW North Dakota campus. Tata advanced India-Singapore subsea.

Ongoing AI hyperscaler demand drives fiber supply ramps (Corning, STL, CommScope, Clearfield, Fujikura tripling production) amid persistent shortages (preforms tight through 2026+). Regional OSP/ISP activity includes Ziply Northwest, Ripple Fiber WA, Ezee Fiber, Brightspeed WiFi 7, Burlington Telecom 5 Gbps, Heartland Fiber Project, FiberLight West Texas, Big Fiber financing, Atomic Fiber Los Alamos, and various BEAD/FTTH builds. Manufacturing focuses on high-count, multi-core, photonics; FBA whitepaper on fiber-AI convergence and power/grid constraints add momentum.

 #FiberForge #FiberOptics #OSPISP

I believe this $AXTI Coherent wafer deal highlights the accelerating demand for indium phosphide photonics components that power AI data center optical interconnects. 

I think it positions the entire sector for strong growth as hyperscalers scale 800G and 1.6T deployments. $COHR benefits directly from secured supply while $LITE, $AAOI, and even broader plays like $AVGO stand to gain from the same ecosystem tailwinds in high-speed transceivers and lasers.

The market will re-rate them sooner than we anticipate 😎

text:
【量子ハードウェア】
Pasqalがカナダで光集積回路（PIC）実装拠点を新設。790万ドル（約11.9億円、1ドル=150円換算）規模で量子・センシング向け製造基盤を強化。
（2026年7月3日 日本時間、Quantum Computing Report）

何が起きたか：
Pasqalは、買収したカナダ子会社Aeponyxを通じて、ケベック州ブロモンのC2MI（MiQro Innovation Collaborative Center）内に、光集積回路（PIC）パッケージングの専門拠点を設立すると発表した。 

このプロジェクトは総額790万ドル（約11.9億円、1ドル=150円換算）で、うち400万ドル（約6.0億円）は連邦・州政府が支援し、その中にはNGen（Next Generation Manufacturing Canada）からの300万ドル（約4.5億円）が含まれる。 

拠点では、シリコンナイトライド（SiN）フォトニクスを活用したPICの実装・組立ラインを構築し、レーザー制御モジュールなど量子コンピュータや量子センシングに必要な光部品の量産体制を整える。初期は試作・少量生産を担い、将来的には年間50万個超のモジュール生産能力を目指す計画だ。HOP Technologies、Phantom Photonicsも参画し、医療センシングやLiDARなど量子以外の用途も視野に入れる。 

読み解き：
今回のポイントは「量子コンピュータを作る」ことではなく、「量子コンピュータを量産できる部品産業を作る」ことにある。

量子コンピュータではチップそのもの以上に、レーザー、光導波路、ファイバー接続などの光学実装が製造ボトルネックになりやすい。特に中性原子方式は多数のレーザーを高精度で制御する必要があり、PICの高品質な実装技術は性能だけでなく生産性にも直結する。 

興味深いのは、この拠点が量子専用工場ではなく、防衛向けLiDARや医療センシング企業も巻き込んだ共通インフラとして設計されている点だ。同じフォトニクス製造設備を複数産業で共有できれば、量子産業単独では難しい設備投資の採算性を高められる。近年は各国が「量子主権」を意識したサプライチェーン整備を進めているが、この発表も製造能力そのものを国内に確保する流れの一例と言える。Pasqalにとっても、Aeponyx買収後の垂直統合を具体的な製造基盤へ発展させる動きとして位置付けられる。 

評価：
・業界インパクト：★★★
・注目度：★★★
・関連銘柄：PASQAL（未上場）

結論：
量子業界ではプロセッサ性能だけでなく、「安定して作れる製造基盤」が競争力になり始めている。今後は各社が光部品やパッケージング、製造拠点への投資をどこまで拡大するかが、商用展開の速度を左右する重要な指標になりそうだ。

text:
【研究開発】
米NSFが量子フォトニクス実用化へ400万ドル追加支援。GPS不要の量子ナビゲーションを視野に、最終段階では最大5,500万ドル規模の量子センター構想へ発展する可能性。
（2026年7月3日 〔日本時間〕・The Quantum Insider）

何が起きたか：
米ミシガン大学が主導する量子フォトニクス研究プロジェクト「QuPID（Quantum Photonic Integration and Deployment）」が、米国立科学財団（NSF）のNational Quantum Virtual Laboratory（NQVL）プログラムのフェーズ2に採択され、2年間で400万ドル（約6億円、1ドル=150円換算）の助成を受けることになった。これはフェーズ1の100万ドル（約1.5億円）に続く資金で、将来的には最大5,500万ドル（約82.5億円）のNSF量子センターへ発展する可能性がある。研究では、量子状態の光を利用した集積フォトニクスチップを開発し、GPSが使えない水中・宇宙・月面などでも利用できる高精度ナビゲーションや超高精度センシングを目指す。プロジェクトにはオハイオ州立大学、ハーバード大学、スタンフォード大学、パデュー大学のほか、Honeywell、TOPTICA Photonics、General Motors、Toyota、Raytheon、NASA Glenn Research Center、Air Force Research Laboratoryなど産学官が参加している。 

読み解き：
このニュースの本質は、「量子コンピュータ」ではなく「量子フォトニクス」の商用化に研究資金の重心が移っている点にある。今回の目標は演算性能ではなく、量子光学を使った実環境向けセンサーやナビゲーションであり、量子技術をインフラへ組み込む流れを象徴している。

特にNSFがフェーズ制で資金を拡大し、最終的には数千万ドル規模の量子センターを構想していることは、単なる大学研究ではなく、量産・実装を前提としたエコシステム形成を狙っていることを示す。さらに、自動車メーカー、防衛企業、NASAまで参加していることから、出口市場が明確な研究テーマとして位置付けられている点も重要だ。

量子業界では計算機だけでなく、量子センシングや量子通信が商用化を先行する可能性が高いと見られており、今回の支援はその流れを裏付ける動きといえる。 

評価：
・業界インパクト：★★★
・注目度：★★★
・関連銘柄：HON, GM, TM

結論：
量子技術の競争軸は計算能力だけではなく、センシングやナビゲーションなど現実世界の用途へ広がりつつある。今後は、このような研究プロジェクトが実証実験や政府調達へ発展するかどうかが、商用化を見極める重要なポイントになる。

text:
The Market That Changed Engines Mid‑Flight Strategic & Tactical Read of the Day 

Today's tape told a story that wasn't subtle: the market didn't break, it pivoted. Momentum the great engine of the 2025–H1 2026 rally finally lost altitude. Semiconductors, memory, photonics, and the AI‑power complex all slipped into a decisive unwind. META admitted AI progress has been slower than expected; photonics rejected their 21‑DMA; semis logged a second straight 5%+ selloff; MTUM printed its sixth rare >3% down day while RSP rose. That's not noise that's regime change.

Yet beneath the surface, the bull market kept breathing. Defensive sectors surged: healthcare, staples, utilities, materials. Equal‑weight advanced. The Dow hit a record. Investors didn't de‑risk they reallocated. This is classic bull‑market rotation: money staying in equities, but choosing different leaders.

Macro reinforced the shift. Payrolls softened, real earnings remain pressured, and factory orders ex‑transport were solid a mix that tempers rate‑hike fears without signaling recession. Micro confirmed it: hyperscalers stabilizing, domestic cyclicals strengthening, and median earnings tracking double‑digit growth. The bottleneck trade is crowded; the broader market is not.

Strategically, leadership is migrating from AI‑momentum to diversified, cash‑flow‑rich, domestically leveraged sectors. Tactically, expect digestion in semis and selective strength in defensives until Q2 earnings clarify whether AI re‑accelerates or hands the baton to the rest of the market.

Bull markets don't die when leaders tire they evolve when new ones emerge.

And to all American readers: Happy Independence Day may your portfolios enjoy some fireworks too. 🇺🇸✨

text:
I saw a few posts claiming $SIVE lasers are not good quality, or not comparable to competitors.

Maybe.

But before treating that as fact, I wanted to start with one simple question:

Who is leading the photonics technology?

As part of this series looking at the people around the table at $SIVE, I looked at Dr. Andrew McKee.

McKee is Managing Director Photonics and CTO, Photonics at Sivers.

He has been with the company since 2001 and was a founding member of CST Global, now Sivers Photonics.

Laser quality is not just one thing.

It is material quality, wavelength control, optical power, thermal behavior, reliability, yield, process repeatability, packaging, and customer qualification.

So when people debate Sivers’ laser quality, the relevant question is:

Do the people leading the photonics roadmap understand the actual physics and manufacturing problems behind III-V laser devices?

In McKee’s case, his background seems directly relevant.

Before Sivers / CST Global, McKee worked at $A, Agilent Technologies, in III-V optoelectronic device design and manufacture.

Plain English:

he worked on semiconductor devices that make, detect, or control light.

That is directly relevant because Sivers Photonics is not building normal silicon logic chips.

It is working in compound-semiconductor photonics, where materials like InP, InGaAsP, GaAs, and AlGaInAs are used because they are much better suited for making lasers.

And the word “manufacture” matters.

A laser company does not win by making one good lab sample.

It wins by repeatedly producing lasers with the right wavelength, power, efficiency, thermal behavior, reliability, lifetime, and yield.

So if the debate is about Sivers’ laser quality, McKee’s background is relevant because it sits directly in the world of III-V laser design and manufacturing.

His publication record is also useful because it translates directly into the “laser quality” debate.

One paper looked at the reliability of DFB lasers used in communications.

a laser is not good just because it turns on once.

For a customer, the real question is whether it keeps working over time, under heat, power, stress, and real operating conditions.

That paper dealt with device specification, failure analysis, and lifetime calculation.

That is basically the engineering version of asking:

How does the laser fail?
Why does it fail?
How long should it last?
Can it meet the reliability needs of the application?

That is directly relevant to Sivers because datacenter and optical-interconnect customers do not just need a bright laser.

They need a laser source that can be qualified, trusted, and manufactured repeatedly.

Another paper studied DFB lasers around 1310 nm.

1310 nm is one of the important wavelength regions used in optical communication.

A DFB laser is designed to emit light at a more controlled wavelength than a simpler laser.

The paper looked at how changing the internal grating structure of the laser changes the wavelength and performance.

That matters because in optical interconnects, wavelength is like a lane on a highway.

If the wavelength is unstable or poorly controlled, the signal can drift, interfere, or become harder to use.

So this connects to one of the key quality questions for Sivers:

Can the laser produce the right wavelength, with enough stability, across real operating conditions?

A more recent silicon-photonics paper looked at bonding InP DFB lasers onto silicon-photonic wafers.

silicon is great for routing and processing optical signals, but it is not naturally good at making light.

InP lasers are good at making light.

So one big challenge in silicon photonics is getting the InP laser and the silicon-photonics platform to work together.

That is not trivial.
The laser has to be placed very accurately.
The light has to couple efficiently into the waveguide.
The device has to manage heat.
The linewidth has to be controlled.
And the system has to be reliable.

That is directly relevant to Sivers because Sivers’ photonics story is about InP laser arrays and light sources used with silicon photonics and optical interconnects.

Overall, the person leading the photonics technology side at $SIVE does not look random.

He appears to have spent decades working on the actual problems that define laser quality.

I have next to no deep knowledge of the actual technology, and I may still be underestimating the complexity of it all.

But from the people side, Andrew McKee looks like exactly the kind of person you would want around the table if the question is whether Sivers Photonics has serious laser engineering capability.

This does not prove Sivers has solved every scaling, yield, or qualification problem.

But it does make me cautious about treating “bad laser quality” as fact without actual technical evidence.

So when people debate Sivers’ laser quality, the relevant question is: Do the people leading the photonics roadmap understand the actual physics and manufacturing problems behind III-V laser devices? In McKee’s case, his background seems directly relevant. Before Sivers / CST Global, McKee worked at $A, Agilent Technologies, in III-V optoelectronic device design and manufacture. Plain English: he worked on semiconductor devices that make, detect, or control light. That is directly relevant because Sivers Photonics is not building normal silicon logic chips. It is working in compound-semiconductor photonics, where materials like InP, InGaAsP, GaAs, and AlGaInAs are used because they are much better suited for making lasers. And the word “manufacture” matters. A laser company does not win by making one good lab sample. It wins by repeatedly producing lasers with the right wavelength, power, efficiency, thermal behavior, reliability, lifetime, and yield. So if the debate is about Sivers’ laser quality, McKee’s background is relevant because it sits directly in the world of III-V laser design and manufacturing. His publication record is also useful because it translates directly into the “laser quality” debate. One paper looked at the reliability of DFB lasers used in communications. a laser is not good just because it turns on once. For a customer, the real question is whether it keeps working over time, under heat, power, stress, and real operating conditions. That paper dealt with device specification, failure analysis, and lifetime calculation. That is basically the engineering version of asking: How does the laser fail? Why does it fail? How long should it last? Can it meet the reliability needs of the application? That is directly relevant to Sivers because datacenter and optical-interconnect customers do not just need a bright laser. They need a laser source that can be qualified, trusted, and manufactured repeatedly. Another paper studied DFB lasers around 1310 nm. 1310 nm is one of the important wavelength regions used in optical communication. A DFB laser is designed to emit light at a more controlled wavelength than a simpler laser. The paper looked at how changing the internal grating structure of the laser changes the wavelength and performance. That matters because in optical interconnects, wavelength is like a lane on a highway. If the wavelength is unstable or poorly controlled, the signal can drift, interfere, or become harder to use. So this connects to one of the key quality questions for Sivers: Can the laser produce the right wavelength, with enough stability, across real operating conditions? A more recent silicon-photonics paper looked at bonding InP DFB lasers onto silicon-photonic wafers. silicon is great for routing and processing optical signals, but it is not naturally good at making light. InP lasers are good at making light. So one big challenge in silicon photonics is getting the InP laser and the silicon-photonics platform to work together. That is not trivial. The laser has to be placed very accurately. The light has to couple efficiently into the waveguide. The device has to manage heat. The linewidth has to be controlled. And the system has to be reliable. That is directly relevant to Sivers because Sivers’ photonics story is about InP laser arrays and light sources used with silicon photonics and optical interconnects. Overall, the person leading the photonics technology side at $SIVE does not look random. He appears to have spent decades working on the actual problems that define laser quality. I have next to no deep knowledge of the actual technology, and I may still be underestimating the complexity of it all. But from the people side, Andrew McKee looks like exactly the kind of person you would want around the table if the question is whether Sivers Photonics has serious laser engineering capability. This does not prove Sivers has solved every scaling, yield, or qualification problem. But it does make me cautious about treating “bad laser quality” as fact without actual technical evidence."}}}, "quoted_status_result": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "core": {"user_results": {"result": {"__typename": "User", "affiliates_highlighted_label": {}, "avatar": {"image_url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/2047933364804395008/gbiwvuj6_normal.jpg"}, "core": {"created_at": "Thu Sep 03 16:38:42 +0000 2009", "name": "Askara", "screen_name": "softmaxedx"}, "dm_permissions": {"can_dm": false}, "follow_request_sent": false, "has_graduated_access": true, "id": "VXNlcjo3MTI5NzA1Nw==", "is_blue_verified": true, "legacy": {"default_profile": false, "default_profile_image": false, "description": "Ex-founder. Bastani answers: Do they understand real semiconductor execution? Karin Raj answers: Do they understand telecom, RF, and customer qualification? Heine answers: Can the financial story become cleaner? Helena answers: Can the board think strategically about M&A, capital markets, and which future deserves capital? The next one is Joakim Nideborn. And I think his question is slightly different: Can the $SIVE story move from pipeline and promise to numbers serious investors can underwrite? Joakim was added to the $SIVE board at the latest AGM. But the more important detail is that he was elected Vice Chairman. Sivers also said he would have primary board oversight for customers, employees, and investors in the Nordic region. That is specific. So I do not read this as just “new board member.” I read it as Sivers adding someone for the translation layer: technology story -> investor language pipeline -> revenue quality assets -> organization acquisitions -> integration growth plan -> cash flow That is Joakim’s lane. He is CFO of $HMS.ST. CFO since 2017. Deputy CEO since 2022. Before that, CFO at Beijer Electronics Group. Before that, management and strategy consulting at Axholmen and Celerant. So the pattern is pretty consistent: industrial technology, finance, operations, strategy, public-company communication. At $HMS.ST, Joakim has been inside a listed Swedish industrial technology company with real products, global customers, hardware/software, acquisitions, production, working capital, margins, and public-market reporting. From HMS’ 2025 annual report: Net sales: 2021: SEK 1.97B 2025: SEK 3.58B Adjusted EBIT: SEK 466M -> SEK 911M Operating cash flow: SEK 508M -> SEK 877M 2025 adjusted operating margin: ~25.5% So he has operated inside a company where industrial technology is translated into margins, cash flow, and public-market discipline. For $SIVE this is highly relevant. The second part is integration. HMS made its two largest acquisitions in 2024: Red Lion Controls in the US. PEAK-System in Germany. In 2025, HMS worked on integrating them into the organization. Red Lion and N-Tron became product brands under HMS. PEAK became part of the New Industries division. HMS also reduced its debt ratio from 3.4x EBITDA at the beginning of the year to 2.2x by year-end, helped by strong cash flow. That is not a small detail. Because $SIVE is also partly an integration story. MixComm brought US mmWave IP. Sivers Photonics / CST brought the Glasgow photonics fab. Wireless depends on manufacturing partners. Photonics needs capacity, qualification, and customer ramp discipline. So the question is not just whether the assets are good. The question is whether they become one coherent company. That is where Joakim’s background becomes interesting. Acquisitions are easy to announce. Integration is where the truth shows up. The third part is Nordic credibility. This may be the most overlooked part of his appointment. Sivers is talking about a potential US listing, PCAOB-related reporting work, and a more international investor base. But it cannot skip the Nordic trust layer. There is a running joke on X that Sweden is unusually good at doubting its own speculative small caps. And honestly, with $SIVE, you can see why that matters. The story has attracted global attention, but the home market still wants the boring proof: clean reporting, clear disclosure, capital discipline, governance, incentives, and whether the pipeline is being translated into numbers. That is why Joakim’s Nordic oversight role matters. Sivers specifically said he would have primary board oversight for customers, employees, and investors in the Nordic region. That is not a throwaway line. He has been CFO of a respected listed Swedish industrial-tech company. So he likely understands the language Nordic investors respond to: less hype, more numbers, more structure, more disclosure, more proof. If $SIVE wants to win trust at home while preparing for a broader international investor base, he is one of the keys."}}}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2067530466337419464"}}, "rest_id": "2068979173910040972", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "24864", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2071713043990368488", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "14248", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
At @OpticaWorldwide  Quantum  Summit in Glasgow, one challenge surfaced repeatedly across discussions  on quantum computing, networking, and photonics: the industry needs  higher accuracy and lower loss.

As light moves from being a communication medium to becoming part of the computational fabric, the performance requirements for photonic circuits  are changing rapidly.

Quantum computing, quantum networking, AI interconnects, and optical processing all depend on increasingly complex optical paths. At that scale, even small imperfections can limit system performance.

This is why the next phase of the industry will not be defined by a single device. It will be defined by the manufacturing platforms capable of supporting the entire quantum and optical computing stack.

In his latest post, our CEO, Ofer Shapiro,  shares why the race among photonic foundries is becoming one of the most important competitions in the quantum industry—and how Quantum Pulse Ventures is approaching this challenge.

#quantum #opticalprocessing #quantumcomputing

text:
According to Lightmatter CEO, Nick Harris, lasers are key and contribute nearly half of the potential revenue in all CPO technology

The industry's transition to CPO from near package optics will accelerate in the coming years, as copper is already reaching its physical limits

For a specialized company like $SIVE in lasers and photonics, this enormously elevates the strategic value of its technology

Every CEO from $LITE to $NVDA etc , is telling you that CPO is just around the corner and that it's going to be generational and inevitable

And you're shorting $AAOI and saying that $SIVE is going to 20 SEK hahahh

I'm not getting off the $SIVE and $AAOI boat

text:
Korea is putting US$1T+ behind #memory capex.

Everyone looks at the big equipment names: $AMAT $KLAC $LRCX.

Fair. BUT

The better question: who sits on the exact HBM chokepoint - less ran, cleaner, more elastic, and still checks the @aleabitoreddit playbook?

That’s why $FORM (#formfactor) is my #1 HBM pick-and-shovel name.

> Bottleneck strength: HIGH; every #HBM wafer needs to be probed before stacking; FORM sits at the test gate where yield risk gets caught early.

> Easy narrative: VERY HIGH; AI needs HBM -> HBM needs yield -> yield needs advanced probe cards. That is the whole pitch.

> Clean pure play: HIGH; advanced probe cards + wafer test exposure, without broad legacy semi-cap drag.

> Big 3 proximity: HIGH; direct exposure to #SKHynix / #Micron / #Samsung HBM ramps, with SK Hynix already naming FORM a “Best Partner.”

> Revenue speed: HIGH; FORM sits upstream of packaging; memory capex converts into orders earlier than many downstream HBM names.

> Growth runway: HIGH; Q1 FY26 already hit record revenue, while 2026–2028 rides HBM4, Texas capacity; estimate $1.6B revenue by 2030.

> Margin expansion: HIGH; gross margin is already near 49%; operating leverage supporting a path toward the 55% long-term target.

> MC elasticity: HIGH; ~$10B market cap; liquid enough for institutions, still small enough for real rerate. 

> Platform optionality: HIGH; HBM = first leg, #Foundry adds the second layer, and advanced packaging + #CPO/#photonics test can extend the story beyond the memory cycle. 

@jukan05 @ParadisLabs @ren_stocks @au_xbt @BULLOFBRITAIN

July Picks: Already Firing

$MU + $DRAM : Closing green. The AI memory trade remains strong. My view: $DRAM has a path to $100+ by late July or August if the memory rally continues.

$AAOI : $132 → $150
$OUST : $42 → $54

$IREN : Still the laggard. EOY target: $120+. July could see a move into the $70–80 range if momentum builds.

$CRDO : I believe this is setting up for a move into the $300s. After the recent pullback from the low $300s, strong revenue growth, AI networking demand, and partnerships across the photonics ecosystem could drive another leg higher.

I am STILL adding more $CRDO & $IREN at these levels.

text:
Researchers in the Bozhi Tian Lab have engineered a wireless, bioinspired "artificial leaf" that harvests light to power next-generation biomedical devices. Published in Nature Photonics, the study by lead author Pengju Li and co-first authors Mengzhan Liufu, Cooper R. Johnston, Young-Woo Pyo, Yuze Zheng, Guangqing Yang introduces a breakthrough nanoplasmonic material—composed of microscopic gold and titanium dioxide structures—that efficiently converts light into high-performance electrical energy. The team successfully used this wireless bioelectronic platform in animal models to pace heartbeats and stimulate sciatic nerves via external light activation, offering a powerful new avenue for target-specific medical therapies like nerve pain management. Beyond biostimulation, the material can even serve as a secure human-computer interface, allowing users to input information via invisible light that is instantly decoded by AI. Read the full story at - https://t.co/Jm0RhAe5VG

text:
$SIVE

Lightmatter CEO Nick Harris: “Lasers are incredibly important. They represent literally half of the revenue opportunity of all of CPO.”

He also expects the industry to move from near-package optics toward CPO over the next several years as copper reaches its physical limits. For a pure-play laser and photonics company like Sivers, this dramatically expands the strategic importance of its technology.
@aleabitoreddit @StormDirac

text:
Buzzberg Daily Alpha.

We scanned feed across X, YouTube and Reddit, Substack .

The cleanest signal was rotation into “next liquid proxies” after AI semis: space, defense drones, humanoid robotics, Korea AI capex, and select compute infrastructure.

1. Space / defense kept attracting the highest-quality flow.

$RKLB  was the strongest space narrative. The thesis is no longer just launches. Speakers framed Rocket Lab as the only public, full-stack space pure play, with the Iridium/spectrum angle creating optionality beyond D2D: IoT, maritime, telemetry, PNT, drones and defense.

$ASTS  was tied to J-LEO subsidy speculation and broader space momentum. $ONDS was framed as a long-term drone/defense compounder, with near-term dilution/warrant risk but Russell inclusion and possible strategic buyer optionality. Drone baskets like $UMAC/$KTOS/$AVAV also came up as “future of warfare” watchlist names.

2. Humanoid robotics moved beyond hype into public-market vehicles.

$CCXI  / Agility Robotics became the main robotics discussion. Bulls like the first US-listed humanoid pure-play angle, Amazon/Nvidia backing, ~$300M backlog, H2 2026 production scaling and RaaS unit economics.

The caution: some speakers openly dislike the SPAC quality/valuation but still expect a speculative run because the public-market wrapper is scarce.

$BOT also came up as a liquid way to own private robotics markups over the next 12-18 months.

3. Korea AI capex was the most important YouTube/macro signal.

Bloomberg and Korean YouTube focused on South Korea’s massive AI infrastructure plan: Samsung/SK Hynix, chip fabs, data centers, renewables and power equipment. The read-through is broad: memory, equipment, power grid, shipbuilding, and domestic Korean suppliers.

Twitter translated that into $MU/$SNDK/DRAM upside overnight. Korean channels also highlighted power equipment and shipbuilders as beneficiaries of AI data-center power shortages and government-backed capex.

4. AI compute relative value is back.

$PENG had the most interesting under-the-radar thesis: possible Middle East sovereign AI breadcrumbs through SK Telecom, MWC 2026 meetings, and Blackwell cluster integration.

$CRWV vs $NBIS was framed as a relative-value trade: CRWV allegedly trades at a large discount to NBIS on 2030 deployed-capacity revenue assumptions, while leverage concerns should fade if revenue scales.

$MSFT also reappeared as a “capex misunderstood” long: Reddit and Twitter both argued the market is penalizing AI buildout the same way it misread Amazon’s reinvestment cycle.

5. Select semis are separating from the weak photonics tape.

Broad optics/photonics was still treated cautiously, but $MXL and $QCOM stood out.

$MXL: Panther/OpenZFS validation at Los Alamos was cited as real proof for the CPU/storage offload thesis.

$QCOM: speakers highlighted automotive design wins, data-center revenue ramp, and HBC/near-memory compute as a lower-power inference architecture.

6. Single-name alpha:

$ABVX was the cleanest biotech catalyst: speakers argued the cancer/NMSC safety concern did not replicate, the label is cleaner, and UC efficacy supports a rerate.

$FCEL stayed alive as a speculative carbon-capture setup tied to the Exxon Rotterdam pilot. One speaker sold tactically to rotate into robotics, but the core FCEL thesis was not described as broken.

$BB had a strong QNX/software thread: autos, robotics, industrial automation, Canada/Japan trade mission deals, and high-margin software model.

$SMCI was the clearest avoid: Taiwan raids, chip-smuggling probe, accounting history, auditor issues. The message was simple: governance risk overwhelms AI exposure.

$UMAC Unusual Machines, $RCAT Red Cat Holdings and $LASE Laser Photonics continue to ride the US drone manufacturing policy push.

The Trump administration's program to fund US drone makers (cutting reliance on Chinese DJI components) is now extending into defense procurement. Laser Photonics' anti-drone system was selected by the US Department of War earlier this month.

Yeah France has a good squad but have you heard of the bottleneck bros?

Unbeatable team ⚡️ 

> @Frenchie_ French buy-side specialist. And a goat for getting us together.

text:
Millimeter-wave (mmWave) technology is powering the next generation of ultra-fast wireless communication. Discover innovative antennas, electro-optic modulators, and key components driving 5G and beyond. 

https://t.co/TIc1uCneZN

#5G #mmWave #Photonics #Wireless #Telecom https://t.co/pZV8Uzeg9y

$IQE
One of the most important points from FY2025, in my view, was that IQE is seeing increasing customer demand for 6/8-inch GaSb-based epiwafers, while its LWIR product has achieved production qualification with a Tier 1 customer.

I believe infrared-based LWIR/MWIR, and potentially SWIR as well, could become a very important growth pillar for IQE through 2030.

IQE’s sensing portfolio can broadly be divided into four platforms:

GaAs: VCSEL / 3D sensing
InP: SWIR / photonics
GaN: LiDAR drivers / microLED
GaSb: IR detectors / T2SL / MWIR / LWIR

In other words, IQE is a rare pure-play merchant epitaxy foundry that owns all the key III-V platforms: GaAs, InP, GaN, and GaSb.

Among these, the area I am particularly focused on is GaSb-based MWIR/LWIR.

Robotics, drones, autonomous driving, and defence systems are still in the early stages of growth.

Today, many systems combine RGB cameras, LiDAR, radar, ToF, and other sensors, but as these systems become more advanced, sensing will not end with RGB cameras alone.

RGB cameras are extremely powerful in the visible spectrum.

They are excellent for recognizing colour, shape, text, and surface-level information.

However, visible-light-based data has clear limitations.

At night, in smoke, dust, fog, strong backlight, camouflage, and adverse weather, RGB cameras alone cannot provide sufficiently robust information.

Future robots, drones, and unmanned systems will not simply need to “see” what is visible.

They will also need to understand the thermal information emitted by people, vehicles, engines, batteries, and industrial equipment.

This is where MWIR/LWIR becomes increasingly important.

MWIR is linked to high-temperature objects, engines, missiles, long-range surveillance, aerospace, and defence systems.

LWIR is linked to people, animals, machine heat, night surveillance, thermal imaging, and hazardous-environment perception.

In other words, MWIR/LWIR may not merely be a “supporting sensor” for future autonomous systems.

In certain environments, it could become a core perception layer that is even more important than RGB.

This is especially true in defence.

The future battlefield is moving toward drones, unmanned vehicles, surveillance systems, automatic target recognition, and night operations.

In these environments, high-resolution RGB images alone are not enough.

To detect people in darkness, vehicles with residual engine heat, hidden equipment, missile-launch heat signatures, or abnormal machine heat, MWIR/LWIR thermal sensing is required.

The same logic applies to robotics.

If humanoids or industrial robots only operate in bright indoor environments, RGB + depth sensors may be enough.

But once they expand into dark factories, warehouses, night patrol, disaster sites, military robotics, and machine overheating detection, the story changes.

For robots, MWIR/LWIR can become both “eyes that see at night” and a “sense that detects heat.”

The problem is that existing MWIR/LWIR sensors have been very expensive.

High-performance IR detectors have been costly because of small substrates, complex epitaxy, low yields, ROIC hybrid bonding, cryocoolers, and expensive optics.

As a result, they have mainly been used in markets that can absorb the cost, such as defence, aerospace, and specialised industrial applications.

But IQE’s 6/8-inch GaSb-based epiwafer directly targets one of these bottlenecks.

When wafer size increases, it is not simply about making the wafer bigger.

The number of dies per wafer increases, large-area manufacturing productivity improves, and there is room for better uniformity and yield.

This creates the foundation for producing high resolution FPAs focal plane arrays more economically.

Simply put, high performance thermal sensing, which was previously too expensive and limited mainly to defence and specialised applications, may have the potential to move into broader markets through large diameter epi platforms.

IQE is not talking about this as a simple R&D project.

It has mentioned both Tier 1 production qualification for its LWIR product and increasing demand for 6/8-inch GaSb-based epiwafers.

As humanoid physical AI models mature and need more sensing vectors to become robust against real-world noise, I believe there will come a point where additional modalities become necessary.

In autonomous driving, this could happen even earlier, with MWIR/LWIR potentially being integrated sooner.

The fact that production qualification has already been completed with a Tier 1 customer suggests that this demand is not theoretical anymore. It may already be starting.

Beyond data centres, how do you view IQE’s future in robotics and autonomous driving?

I believe infrared-based LWIR/MWIR, and https://t.co/i9JJYxmBIt", "id_str": "2071277131095818361", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "en", "possibly_sensitive": false, "possibly_sensitive_editable": true, "quote_count": 0, "reply_count": 3, "retweet_count": 5, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "1603757368923197443"}, "note_tweet": {"is_expandable": true, "note_tweet_results": {"result": {"entity_set": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [{"indices": [0, 4], "text": "IQE"}], "urls": [], "user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNzEyNzcxMzA2NzIxNzMwNTY=", "media": {"inline_media": []}, "richtext": {"richtext_tags": []}, "text": "$IQE One of the most important points from FY2025, in my view, was that IQE is seeing increasing customer demand for 6/8-inch GaSb-based epiwafers, while its LWIR product has achieved production qualification with a Tier 1 customer. I believe infrared-based LWIR/MWIR, and potentially SWIR as well, could become a very important growth pillar for IQE through 2030. IQE’s sensing portfolio can broadly be divided into four platforms: GaAs: VCSEL / 3D sensing InP: SWIR / photonics GaN: LiDAR drivers / microLED GaSb: IR detectors / T2SL / MWIR / LWIR In other words, IQE is a rare pure-play merchant epitaxy foundry that owns all the key III-V platforms: GaAs, InP, GaN, and GaSb. Among these, the area I am particularly focused on is GaSb-based MWIR/LWIR. Robotics, drones, autonomous driving, and defence systems are still in the early stages of growth. Today, many systems combine RGB cameras, LiDAR, radar, ToF, and other sensors, but as these systems become more advanced, sensing will not end with RGB cameras alone. RGB cameras are extremely powerful in the visible spectrum. They are excellent for recognizing colour, shape, text, and surface-level information. However, visible-light-based data has clear limitations. At night, in smoke, dust, fog, strong backlight, camouflage, and adverse weather, RGB cameras alone cannot provide sufficiently robust information. Future robots, drones, and unmanned systems will not simply need to “see” what is visible. They will also need to understand the thermal information emitted by people, vehicles, engines, batteries, and industrial equipment. This is where MWIR/LWIR becomes increasingly important. MWIR is linked to high-temperature objects, engines, missiles, long-range surveillance, aerospace, and defence systems. LWIR is linked to people, animals, machine heat, night surveillance, thermal imaging, and hazardous-environment perception. In other words, MWIR/LWIR may not merely be a “supporting sensor” for future autonomous systems. In certain environments, it could become a core perception layer that is even more important than RGB. This is especially true in defence. The future battlefield is moving toward drones, unmanned vehicles, surveillance systems, automatic target recognition, and night operations. In these environments, high-resolution RGB images alone are not enough. To detect people in darkness, vehicles with residual engine heat, hidden equipment, missile-launch heat signatures, or abnormal machine heat, MWIR/LWIR thermal sensing is required. The same logic applies to robotics. If humanoids or industrial robots only operate in bright indoor environments, RGB + depth sensors may be enough. But once they expand into dark factories, warehouses, night patrol, disaster sites, military robotics, and machine overheating detection, the story changes. For robots, MWIR/LWIR can become both “eyes that see at night” and a “sense that detects heat.” The problem is that existing MWIR/LWIR sensors have been very expensive. High-performance IR detectors have been costly because of small substrates, complex epitaxy, low yields, ROIC hybrid bonding, cryocoolers, and expensive optics. As a result, they have mainly been used in markets that can absorb the cost, such as defence, aerospace, and specialised industrial applications. But IQE’s 6/8-inch GaSb-based epiwafer directly targets one of these bottlenecks. When wafer size increases, it is not simply about making the wafer bigger. The number of dies per wafer increases, large-area manufacturing productivity improves, and there is room for better uniformity and yield. This creates the foundation for producing high resolution FPAs focal plane arrays more economically. Simply put, high performance thermal sensing, which was previously too expensive and limited mainly to defence and specialised applications, may have the potential to move into broader markets through large diameter epi platforms. IQE is not talking about this as a simple R&D project. It has mentioned both Tier 1 production qualification for its LWIR product and increasing demand for 6/8-inch GaSb-based epiwafers. As humanoid physical AI models mature and need more sensing vectors to become robust against real-world noise, I believe there will come a point where additional modalities become necessary. In autonomous driving, this could happen even earlier, with MWIR/LWIR potentially being integrated sooner. The fact that production qualification has already been completed with a Tier 1 customer suggests that this demand is not theoretical anymore. It may already be starting. Beyond data centres, how do you view IQE’s future in robotics and autonomous driving?"}}}, "rest_id": "2071277131095818361", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "2032", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Die Verlagerung der Halbleitersicherungsqualität von Siliziumkarbid hin zur optischen KI Infrastruktur

Der explosive Anstieg der Rechenlasten in modernen Rechenzentren verändert die Anforderungen an die physische Zuverlässigkeit von Mikrochips grundlegend. Da der Ausfall einzelner Komponenten in massiven KI Clustern immense finanzielle Schäden verursacht, rücken spezialisierte Verfahren zur Qualitätsprüfung direkt auf Wafer-Ebene ins Zentrum der globalen Wertschöpfungskette.
Das US Halbleiterprüfunternehmen Aehr Test Systems demonstriert diese transformationale Dynamik in der ersten Hälfte des Jahres 2026 durch einen beispiellosen Kursanstieg an den Finanzmärkten. Die Aktie, die zu Beginn des Jahres noch nahe der 20 US Dollar Marke notierte, kletterte im Juni 2026 zeitweise auf über 115 US Dollar. Dieser Zuwachs spiegelt den erfolgreichen strategischen Wandel des Unternehmens wider. Während Aehr Test Systems in den vergangenen Jahren primär von Systemen für Siliziumkarbid-Leistungshalbleiter im stagnierenden Elektromobilitätssektor abhing, positioniert sich der Konzern nun als kritischer Infrastrukturpartner für die optische Datenübertragung in KI Rechenzentren.
Den technologischen Hebel bildet die proprietäre FOX-XP Plattform, ein vollautomatisiertes System für das sogenannte Wafer-Level Burn-In. Bei diesem Verfahren werden hunderte Halbleiter-Wafer gleichzeitig unter extremen thermischen und elektrischen Bedingungen künstlich gealtert, um fehlerhafte Bauteile noch vor dem finalen Verpackungsprozess zu identifizieren. Ein entscheidender Engpass moderner Beschleuniger-Cluster liegt in der Kommunikation über optische Transceiver (Silicon Photonics), die Lichtsignale statt elektrischer Impulse nutzen. Da diese optischen Schnittstellen extrem anfällig für frühe Ausfälle sind, verlangen führende Hyperscaler eine lückenlose Vorabprüfung.
Die finanzielle Bestätigung dieser Neuausrichtung lieferten die jüngsten Auftragseingänge. Nach einem vorangegangenen, operativ schwächeren Quartal mit lediglich 10,3 Millionen US Dollar Umsatz meldete Aehr für die zweite Jahreshälfte 2026 einen massiven Sprung bei den Buchungen auf über 92 Millionen US Dollar. Treiber war eine Serie von Großaufträgen, darunter eine Rekordbestellung über 41 Millionen US Dollar von einem führenden Hyperscale-KI Kunden sowie Folgeaufträge eines globalen Marktführers für Netzwerklösungen. Diese Systeme dienen sowohl der technologischen Qualifikation neuer Chip-Designs als auch der anschließenden Hochvolumenproduktion.
Trotz dieser operativen Meilensteine bleibt die Aktie an den Kapitalmärkten von hoher Volatilität geprägt, was sich in einer temporären Korrektur Ende Juni auf rund 91 US Dollar äußerte. Mit einem Kurs-Umsatz-Verhältnis von über 60 reflektiert die Bewertung bereits erhebliche Vorschusslorbeeren auf die kommenden Fiskaljahre. Da das Unternehmen den kapitalintensiven Hochlauf zudem über ein aktives Aktienplatzierungsprogramm (At-The-Market) mitfinanziert, müssen die anstehenden Quartalszahlen eine fehlerfreie Skalierung der Auslieferungen belegen. Für den Technologiesektor untermauert der Fall Aehr jedoch ein fundamentales Prinzip: Der wirtschaftliche Erfolg im KI Zyklus verschiebt sich zunehmend von den reinen Chip-Designern hin zu den hochspezialisierten Enablern, welche die physische Betriebssicherheit des Gesamtsystems garantieren.

text:
$INFQ recent contract / funding momentum:

U.S. Navy : $1M contract for QuIRC, Infleqtion’s quantum-inspired AI software for RF signal processing in contested environments.
DARPA HARQ: $2M contract to develop Multistaq, a software platform for heterogeneous quantum systems across multiple qubit types.
NASA JPL: $20M in contracts to date for the Quantum Gravity Gradiometer Pathfinder mission, where Infleqtion is helping build space-based quantum sensing tech.
U.S. Department of Commerce / CHIPS Office: proposed $100M funding LOI to accelerate U.S. neutral-atom quantum computing, quantum hardware, photonics, and full-stack system development.
Important distinction: the $100M CHIPS item is not a finalized contract yet, but it is proposed, milestone-based funding subject to final documents and approvals. 

My Friday brain says: small announced contract dollars today, but strong validation from Navy, DARPA, NASA, DOE, and Commerce. $INFQ is building government credibility in quantum computing, sensing, defense, and space.
You know I love me some space.

text:
$IONQ

A recent report by Kearney makes it clear that trapped ions are the most promising way of building commercially useful quantum computers.

The report discusses these architectures:

1/ Trapped ions

Highest fidelity but slower gate speeds

All-to-all connectivity leads to lower physical qubit overhead

Best from unit-economics perspective

Bottom line: It’s the most commercially viable quantum computers. You’ll get high quality data when using them but have to wait for your calculations longer.

2/ Superconducting qubits

Fast gate times

Higher physical qubit overhead than for trapped ions

But even under optimistic assumptions, scaling will make system costs unsustainable

Report actually calls this an “existential architectural threat”

Bottom line: There’s a good chance you’ll never see a useful quantum computer built on millions of superconducting qubits

3/ Neutral atoms

High fidelity

All-to-all connectivity leading to lower physical qubit overhead like for trapped ions

As promising as trapped-ion technology but real challenge is engineering: Calibration automation, system resets, and error correction

Bottom line: Definitely has potential to scale up but not clear how engineering challenges will be overcome

4/ Photonics

Combines two advantages: Room-temperature operation and compatibility with foundries currently building semiconductor chips

Biggest bet is modularity, which means you break quantum computers into small interconnected processing units

Biggest challenge is scaling because photons don’t naturally interact with each other and light is subject to losses

Bottom line: Not clear if this manufacturing leverage will help scale photonics.

The conclusion of the report speaks for itself:

“The architectures most likely to lead are therefore the ones that can repeatedly translate physics progress into operationally stable, cost-improving systems, because the market will ultimately reward not “qubits on a slide,” but sustained improvement in error-corrected capability per dollar.

Based on Kearney analysis in collaboration with leading quantum companies, industry and academic experts, trapped-ion quantum computers are early leaders to be the most cost-effective quantum computer at fault tolerant scale.”

Long $IONQ.

text:
“… the tape is treating a triple-digit grower in the middle of the photonics boom like the thesis fell apart, and it did not. The orders are signed, and the factories are going up.

Everyone got scared right as it got cheap.”

$AAOI
embedded_text:
AAOI is trading at about 2x what the company itself expects to do in 2027. For a stock growing triple digits, that looks pretty rich.

The whole photonics group got hit the last couple of weeks, and almost none of it was about the companies. Co-packaged optics delay scares https://t.co/x9DZoQTDoy

Co-packaged optics delay scares spooked people, the Fed turned hawkish, the memory names cracked, and the high-beta optical stocks got dumped with everything else. Classic crowded trade unwinding. Nothing actually changed in the businesses. So look at what you are getting at $130. The market cap is around $10.6 billion. Management's own target for mid-2027 is about $471 million a month in data center revenue, or roughly $5.6 billion a year. That puts the stock at about 2x that target today. Slower photonics names trade richer than that on the numbers they have already done. And this is not a hope and a prayer. Full-year guidance is $1.1 billion, more than double last year. Q1 grew 51%. They have over $324 million in locked 800G and 1.6T orders. They are expanding laser capacity by around 350% by the end of 2027. Supply is expected to trail demand the whole way through. They start shipping their big 1.6T order on July 1. Rosenblatt sees $220. The one fair knock is that management has to actually hit the numbers, and they have been lumpy in the past. That is the real risk, and I am not going to pretend it away. But the tape is treating a triple-digit grower in the middle of the photonics boom like the thesis fell apart, and it did not. text:
Quantum Computing Inc. $QUBT just made one of its biggest strategic moves—and it’s about far more than buying another company.

The acquisition of NHanced Semiconductors signals that QUBT is shifting from being a company with promising quantum technology to one that wants control over manufacturing and commercialization.

That difference matters.

Many quantum companies have brilliant science but depend on third-party manufacturers to turn prototypes into real products. QUBT is trying to shorten that gap.

The deal is structured intelligently.

• $68.1M cash upfront
• $5M in QUBT stock
• Up to $72M in earnout payments only if NHanced achieves ambitious revenue and EBITDA targets during 2027-2028.
• $20M remains in escrow, protecting QUBT if performance milestones aren’t reached.

This isn’t simply buying technology.

It’s buying execution, while making sellers earn the highest valuation through future performance.

For long-term investors, that’s a positive signal.

NHanced brings semiconductor packaging, advanced integration, and manufacturing capabilities that could become critical as QUBT develops photonic quantum computing, quantum sensing, AI hardware, and integrated optical chips.

The bigger picture is even more interesting.

The future AI stack won’t just require better software—it will require new hardware.

Quantum computing, silicon photonics, optical interconnects, advanced semiconductor packaging, and AI accelerators are increasingly converging into one ecosystem.

Owning more of that value chain gives QUBT greater control over product quality, production timelines, margins, intellectual property, and customer relationships.

However, investors should remain realistic.

The acquisition alone doesn’t prove commercial success.

QUBT still needs to demonstrate that NHanced can generate meaningful revenue growth and that the combined business can manufacture products at scale.

The required financial statements and pro-forma results have not yet been released, so it’s still too early to fully judge whether the purchase price represents a bargain or an expensive bet.

Meanwhile, the recent criticism surrounding Microsoft’s topological quantum research serves as another reminder of how early this industry still is.

Scientific breakthroughs remain uncertain.

Engineering remains difficult.

Commercial adoption will likely take years.

That’s why companies with real manufacturing capabilities may eventually separate themselves from companies relying solely on research papers.

The next phase of quantum computing won’t be won by the company with the best theory.

It will likely be won by the company that can design, manufacture, scale, and deliver commercially viable quantum hardware.

QUBT is betting that controlling more of its manufacturing pipeline today will give it a stronger competitive position tomorrow.

text:
Everyone is asking which AI company will win.

I think that is the wrong question.

The real question is:

who sells the physical bottlenecks that every AI winner will be forced to consume?

Compute.
Energy.
Grid.
Cooling.
Semiconductors.
Photonics.
Silicon.
Uranium.
HALEU.
Isotopes.
Nuclear fuel.
Advanced materials.
AI may not be a bubble.
But most AI companies will die.
The internet changed the world, but most dot-com stocks disappeared.
The same can happen again.
That is why I am not only watching the obvious names like NVDA, TSM, ASML and AVGO.
I am watching the invisible infrastructure:
ASPI — isotopes, Silicon-28, HALEU
LEU — uranium enrichment / HALEU
CEG — nuclear power
BWXT — nuclear components
VRT — power and cooling
ETN — electrical infrastructure
PWR — grid construction
GEV — grid and energy systems
ALAB — AI connectivity
COHR / LITE — photonics
BESI — advanced packaging
SMR / OKLO — advanced nuclear
FLNC / POWL / AMSC — grid, storage and power equipment
ORA / FRVO — geothermal firm power
GFUZ / TAE — fusion as the long-duration frontier
I may be early.
I may be wrong on some names.
But I refuse to only chase the application layer while the real war is moving into electricity, materials, nuclear fuel, interconnects and supply chains.
The future of AI will not be decided only by models.

It will be decided by the physical infrastructure that allows intelligence to exist at scale.

I am not looking for hype.

I am looking for bottlenecks. Bye . #AIBottlenecks

#AIInfrastructure #EnergyWar

I was scrolling trough my feed today, and the only thing I see is people comparing Quantum (2025) to photonics. 

Yeah we seen a very good run for quantum and then it just dumped. But I know why it dumped. Because THERE WAS NO REAL REVENUE. 

Comparing to photonics names like $AAOI they have real revenue and huge projections. So don’t compare some temporary bs to something real.

text:
$COHR $LITE $AAOI 

The recent hammering of photonics/CPO names, which was partly triggered by the SemiAnalysis note pushing timelines out for CPO adoption, looks like a short-sighted overreaction rather than a collapse in the fundamental thesis imo.

The realistic path is to use copper for scale-up as long as you can on short-reach, low-latency links inside the rack/cluster, and then move to optics as the final phase of the transition. The move to optics is fundamentally necessary. Copper interconnects eventually hit physical limits w/ exponentially rising power draw, signal degradation, and restricted reach as AI clusters scale to massive sizes w/ extreme bandwidth demands. Copper simply cannot efficiently move data across longer distances and at the densities required w/o consuming unsustainable power or compromising performance. 

This hybrid, phased reality is not a bear case. Copper staying relevant longer actually extends the runway and de-risks CPO adoption as clusters scale to a point where copper hits hard walls.

We’re still early in the multi-year AI infrastructure buildout. The “slow ramp” narrative today is exactly what you should be expect anyways before the volumes compound in the back half of the decade.

text:
Man, seeing semis get hammered again on what’s basically old Meta news is wild. I think Bloomberg just loves stirring the pot, UBS called it right, Zuckerberg talked about selling capacity ages ago. Those $48B neocloud deals don’t scream overbuild to me. I believe the real AI demand is still there, so I’m eyeing dips in $AAOI and $MRVL.

I’ve seen this movie before with the AMD/Nvidia export scare earlier this year. Markets overreact every time. I think this is weak hands folding while the big picture (power shortages, compute needs) hasn’t changed. Personally believe loading up on the AI supply chain names like $AEHR and photonics plays here could pay off big once the noise dies.

Honestly, semis trade on headlines more than reality short term. I believe this selloff is overdone, $META optimizing capacity isn’t the same as slashing AI spend. I think we’re getting a gift on $AAOI $SIVEF $LITE, $TER, etc. Staying long the thesis, volatility like this separates the believers from the rest.
@kaiseninvest Photonics cycle over aparantly.

text:
Dear @vonderleyen,
How wonderful that you and the entire College of Commissioners travelled to Cork today to visit the Tyndall National Institute! A real “innovation powerhouse,” as you so aptly put it. “From test to scale, from ideas to industry” – that sounds like proper European future music. And Ireland really is a beautiful country. The green hills, the Guinness, the friendly people… a business trip like that must be quite enjoyable. The European taxpayer is surely delighted that you’re all so motivated and on the road.
Just one small, modest question out of pure fiscal curiosity: Did you know that right on your doorstep in Leuven, Belgium, there is an institute that is considered the global benchmark in exactly this field – integrated semiconductors, nanoelectronics, photonics and deep-tech hardware?
imec employs over 6,500 scientists, engineers and innovators from more than 100 countries. That’s more than ten times the size of Tyndall (which had a proud 580 staff and students in 2024, including 173 postgraduates with 158 PhD students). imec was Belgium’s top patent applicant in 2024/2025 and is driving major European Chips Act pilot lines. While Tyndall is getting an expansion worth over €100 million and works with around 200 industry partners, imec has had a global network for years and generates well over a billion euros in revenue annually – mostly through direct industry collaborations.
But of course: why stay in Leuven when you can fly the whole College, security teams, protocol staff and all the rest to Ireland instead? The taxpayers in Germany, France, Italy and all the other member states are happy to pay for a bit of extra European unity – plus flights, hotels, escorted convoys and the entire logistical effort for a visit to Cork, when Leuven is only about a half-hour drive from Brussels.
We really do think it’s nice that you praise Tyndall so much. The institute has been doing good work since it was founded in 2004 (with roots in the NMRC from 1981). But if you’re going to celebrate European top-tier research, then please do it where it’s biggest, most established and closest. It would save money – and CO₂. The Irish government can still be happy anyway – they’ve already received plenty of EU funds.
With friendly but slightly puzzled regards,

Value gap & trap of Photonics as it stands now.

$GLW - A clear moat on glass. They lead in both Data Center for high speed data transfer & Gorilla glass. A clear moat. Verdict: Value gap. 

$COHR, $LITE, $KEYS - A loyal sub-ordinates with strong guidance. They need to prove but heading in right direction. 

$CIEN $AAOI - Extremely overvalued, no moat. Verdict: Value trap. Morning 

There is a lot to prove for many of these companies. Some will stay, some will go. Time will tell us.

text:
Touching on our report from last night.

laser enrichment ( $SILXY $QLE $ASPI ) demand will accrue to specialty mid‑IR systems (CO₂/Raman, QCL, etc.), 

$SIVE thesis remains about AI datacenter ELS, CPO/foundry‑stack option value, and InP sovereignty rather than this enrichment TAM.

The crossover, is “collateral beneficiary”: sovereign InP and RF‑photonics infrastructure that sits next to, and communicates with, enrichment assets rather than trying to sell the enrichment lasers themselves.

Much more to come.

text:
COHR makes the InP lasers and optical components that go into both current pluggable transceivers and next-generation co-packaged designs.

TSEM has silicon photonics foundry capability - the integration layer that allows optical components to be manufactured on silicon substrates compatible with chip packaging processes.

Both positions have an explicit line into the transition that's happening over the next 2-4 years.

Own the bottlenecks, not the apps.

https://t.co/02QOL7h7hn
@EmmanuelSimond @Frenchie_ Tldr : sell photonics, buy TSMC

text:
Nobody is saying Physics = Engineering. The point is that careers evolve.

I studied Physics in undergraduate, earned an MSc in Optics and Photonics from one of Germany’s leading optics programs 🇩🇪, and today I work as an engineer in the semiconductor industry after years of specialized education and training. Engineering requires engineering competence, but that competence is not always acquired through a single undergraduate degree.

I’m not fully familiar with NSE/COREN’s criteria, but in my case, my MSc was essentially an engineering degree focused on optical systems, lasers, photonic devices, and related technologies. A Physics degree was considered an appropriate prerequisite for admission because of its strong mathematical and scientific foundation.

There is no area of optics or photonics engineering where someone is automatically more competent simply because they earned an undergraduate degree in Mechanical or Electrical Engineering. Expertise in this field comes from specialized education, research, and professional experience not from the title of your first degree.

Engineering is also incredibly broad. Having a bachelor’s degree in Electrical or Mechanical Engineering does not automatically make someone a Photonics, Optical, Aerospace, Biomedical, or Semiconductor Engineer. Those are specialized disciplines that require additional training.

This discussion isn’t about bypassing engineering education. It’s about recognizing professionals who have acquired equivalent engineering competence through advanced study, rigorous training, and industry experience. Many countries and employers assess competence based on what you know and can do not solely on the name of your undergraduate degree. That’s the conversation worth having.

text:
From Grok: Microgravity (and to a lesser extent, the vacuum and radiation environment of space) enables manufacturing processes that are hindered on Earth by gravity-induced effects like sedimentation, buoyancy-driven convection, and structural deformation. This leads to more uniform, defect-free, or otherwise superior materials that can be worth the high cost of launch and return for high-value, low-mass products.31
Here are the main categories of things that can be best manufactured (higher quality/performance) or, in some cases, only practically manufactured in space:
1. High-Quality Protein and Small-Molecule Crystals for Pharmaceuticals
•Why space? In microgravity, there’s no sedimentation or convection currents, so crystals grow more slowly and uniformly, forming larger, more ordered structures with fewer defects. This improves drug formulation, delivery (e.g., better-controlled release particles), stability, and enables structure determination via X-ray crystallography for new drug design.35
•Examples: Protein crystals (e.g., for Merck’s Keytruda), small-molecule drugs like ritonavir (HIV medication). Companies like Varda Space have flown missions producing these.4
•Status: One of the most commercially promising near-term areas due to high value-per-gram.
2. Advanced Optical Fibers (Especially ZBLAN)
•Why space? On Earth, gravity causes crystallization defects and imperfections during fiber drawing from molten glass. Microgravity suppresses these, yielding fibers with far lower signal loss (potentially orders of magnitude better).32
•Example: ZBLAN (zirconium-barium-lanthanum-sodium-aluminum fluoride glass) fibers for infrared transmission, lasers, medical imaging, and high-speed communications. Companies like Flawless Photonics have produced kilometers of it on the ISS.49
•Potential: Could revolutionize telecom, sensing, and medical applications.
3. Semiconductors, Electronic Crystals, and Related Materials
•Why space? Reduced convection and sedimentation allow more homogeneous, defect-free crystal growth and layered structures.
•Examples: Semiconductor wafers, scintillator crystals for radiation detection, improved thin films, and electrode materials. Also, potential for better solar cells or quantum materials.35
4. Uniform Alloys, Metals, and Foams
•Why space? Diffusion dominates over gravity-driven separation/sedimentation, leading to more uniform microstructures. Containerless processing (levitation) is easier. Metal foams can form without collapsing.37
•Examples: Specialized alloys with superior properties; metal foams for lightweight, high-strength applications (though some foams are made on Earth, microgravity can enable better uniformity).11
5. Biological and Medical Products (Tissues, Cells, Devices)
•Why space? No gravity-induced slumping or uneven layering in 3D structures; better nutrient diffusion and cell organization.
•Examples: Artificial retinas, engineered tissues, pluripotent stem cells, bio-inks for bioprinting, and drug-delivery devices. Microgravity helps mature cardiac cells or create delicate structures.34
6. Precision Spheres and Micro-Encapsulation
•Why space? Surface tension naturally forms perfect spheres in zero-g without gravitational deformation.
•Examples: Monodisperse latex spheres (used as calibration standards; early commercial space product), perfect ball bearings, hollow spheres, or microcapsules for drugs.39
7. Other Advanced Materials and Processes
•Specialized glass and ceramics: More uniform or complex shapes without slumping (e.g., via additive manufacturing).20
•Containerless processing: High-purity materials melted and solidified without crucible contamination.

text:
The next big bottleneck 👇

Right now, the market is rushing into semiconductor stocks. But it's not looking at the big, famous companies everyone knows. Instead, investors are chasing quick trends to find AI bottlenecks...

Just look at what recently happened with RAM and photonics.

I think the next big bottleneck we will face in the coming months is energy. AI needs a lot of power. Companies like BWXT Technologies, American Electric Power, and Schneider Electric could win big from this. ⚡

text:
Navellier & Associates: $NDX long term up!
You go all the way out to 1 year, the NASDAQ is 33% higher 100% of the time.
Jason Bodner argues that despite recent market volatility, institutional 'big money' is still flowing into stocks, particularly into AI-related bottleneck plays like memory (Micron) and photonics. He sees the severe memory shortage lasting through 2028, compressing forward PEs via earnings growth. He also highlights space infrastructure (Carpenter Tech, Vertiv, Coherent) as structural beneficiaries and notes that equal-weight indices are outperforming as leadership shifts from mega-caps to growing companies.
#Nasdaq #TechStocks #AIStocks #MegaCaps

text: Fair point — and I completely agree, the average investor shouldn’t be running 20-30  positions. This portfolio exists for a specific purpose: to track, analyse and share ideas across multiple themes so subscribers can see the full landscape and pick what fits their own strategy and risk tolerance. My job is to do the research across all these themes so you don’t have to — you take what resonates, build conviction in 5-10 names maximum, and size appropriately for your own portfolio. If you’re asking my highest conviction names right now — $SIVEF for asymmetric photonics upside, $MRVL as the core AI semiconductor compounder, $KEEL for NeoCloud infrastructure exposure, $CRDO for proven high-speed connectivity revenue, and $GOOG as the anchor and long-term compounder — but even these five, please do your own research, understand the thesis behind each name, and only invest what you can afford to lose. Nobody’s top 5 should be your top 5 without your own conviction behind it.
